chapters.summary: Council staff reminds attendees to silence electronic devices, not approach the dais, and sign up for public testimony with the Sergeant-at-Arms.
chapters.title: Staff housekeeping and testimony sign-up instructions

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council hearing from the Committee on Veteran Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, I'd like to remind everyone to please silence their electronic devices and at no point is anyone to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you would like to sign up for public testimony and have not done so already, feel free to sign a slip located outside in the hallway with the sergeant arms.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we're ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Frank Morano opens the hearing, introduces himself as chair of the Veterans Committee, and thanks attendees joining in person, via Zoom, and on cable television.
chapters.title: Chair Morano opens hearing and welcomes attendees

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I am Councilman Frank Moreno.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I have the pleasure and honor of chairing the Veterans Committee of the New York City Council.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank everybody who's joining us here in person.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank everybody who's watching via Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank everybody that's watching on cable television when there's nothing else on.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But I really want to thank everybody for tuning in, however they're tuning in, for such an important topic.

chapters.summary: Morano introduces the oversight topic of programs and services for LGBTQ+ veterans, emphasizing that veterans' issues should be nonpartisan and highlighting bipartisan cooperation on the committee.
chapters.title: Morano introduces LGBTQ+ veterans oversight topic and nonpartisan advocacy

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And today, our oversight hearing is on programs and services for LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: When I first took over this committee, I mentioned that, to me, veterans'issues were the one issue that should be and ideally would be totally nonpartisan.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Conservatives love talking about veterans'issues because it deals with patriotism, defending the country, all sorts of things that traditionally conservatives are all about.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: A lot of progressives love focusing on veterans'issues because it gives them an opportunity to talk about health care and housing and all sorts of things that progressives are all about on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And to me, the thing that I love most about this committee is we have members on both sides of the political spectrum, and we are all rowing in the same direction to deliver for New Yorkers and especially for the veterans that have served.

chapters.summary: Morano details unique challenges facing LGBTQ+ veterans including higher rates of PTSD, sexual assault victimization, and lingering effects post-DADT repeal, vowing the committee will not accept the status quo.
chapters.title: Morano on unique challenges facing LGBTQ+ veterans

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Nowhere is that more noticeable than in terms of the issues that are uniquely facing LGBT plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: As our committee has researched and as we'll get into, LGBT veterans have a higher instance of.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: PTSD, LGBT veterans have higher incidence of being victimized by sexual assault,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: and a number of other things that, even after the ending of Don't Ask, Don't Tell, continue to permeate throughout the veterans community.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And this committee, which has some very dedicated council members on it, is not going to just sit by and accept that as the new status quo.

chapters.summary: Morano outlines his goals for the hearing: developing a playbook for legislative action, public awareness, and fixing problems facing LGBTQ+ veterans.
chapters.title: Morano outlines hearing goals for legislative action playbook

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So what I'm hoping we can get at in today's hearing is a playbook, is a playbook for legislative action, a playbook for...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Public awareness, and a way that we can fix what is a very real problem.

chapters.summary: Morano congratulates retired Sergeant First Class Dominic Pepe from Woodhaven for his induction into the New York State Veterans Hall of Fame.
chapters.title: Morano congratulates Sgt. Dominic Pepe for Veterans Hall of Fame induction

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I do want to mention two things that are particularly important, given the circumstances and given our history.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: First, I want to give a big congratulations to retired Sergeant First Class Dominic Pepe from Woodhaven for his induction into the New York State Veterans Hall of Fame.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Not long ago, I did not even know there was a New York State Veterans Hall of Fame.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And to me, Dominic Pepe is exactly the type of person that's

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: this hearing is focused on today, and I'm glad to see that he's been recognized on a statewide basis.

chapters.summary: Morano recounts the legacy of James Jimmy Zappalordi, a gay Vietnam War veteran murdered in Staten Island, whose case became the borough's first officially designated hate crime and inspired the NY State Hate Crimes Act of 2000.
chapters.title: Morano on legacy of James Zappalordi and hate crime activism

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I also want to take a moment to speak about the legacy of a Staten Islander who lives in my district,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: and there's a street named for him six minutes from my office and ten minutes from where I grew up, and that is James Jimmy Zappalordi, a gay Vietnam War veteran who was murdered because of his sexual orientation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: His murder was the first officially designated hate crime in the borough of Staten Island,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: and his plight inspired activism that led to the passage of the New York State Hate Crimes Act of the year 2000, which was signed by Governor Pataki, which allowed harsher penalties for crimes motivated by bias

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: against race, religion, sexual orientation, etc.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And Zappalordi's murder did more than just cause grief.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: His legacy united communities.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It sparked a level of activism that really hadn't been seen before, certainly in the Staten Island South Shore community, certainly in the LGBT community, and certainly in the military veteran community.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: There was the Zappalordi Society, which was founded in 1992, and the Pride Center of Staten Island, which was founded in 2013,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: and all about providing lasting support and services to Staten Island's LGBTQ plus community.

chapters.summary: Morano presents statistics on LGBTQ+ service members discharged between 1980 and 1993 and under DADT, noting over 13,000 were denied benefits, recognition, and dignity despite their service.
chapters.title: Morano on LGBTQ+ veteran discharge statistics under DADT

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let me say a word or two and an observation or two.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: about our LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Between 1980 and 1993, over 19,000 LGBTQ plus service members were discharged,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: many with other than honorable statuses and under don't ask, don't tell, more than 13,000 were denied benefits, recognition and dignity.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And you think about that.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: These people are just in need of the GI Bill.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: These people are just as much in need of health care, of housing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And to think that they were denied anything that should be rightly given to a veteran who has risked their lives in the service of our country, it's abominable.

chapters.summary: Morano discusses ongoing inequities including the 2025 transgender service ban, higher rates of mental health challenges, suicide, PTSD, chronic illness, and military sexual trauma among LGBTQ+ veterans.
chapters.title: Morano on ongoing inequities including trans ban and mental health disparities

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And these policies forced veterans to lose health care, education, and retirement benefits while at the same time enduring discrimination, harassment, and trauma in service to their country.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Even today, inequities persist with about 1,000 transgender service members discharged after the 2025 ban on their service.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: As a result of these policies, as well as wider discrimination and violence against LGBTQ plus individuals,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: LGBTQ plus veterans face higher rates of mental health challenges, suicide, PTSD, and chronic illness.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They're also significantly more likely to experience military sexual trauma and assault.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: further compounding a lot of these health issues.

chapters.summary: Morano highlights major data gaps, noting 89% of VA records lack gender identity information, making LGBTQ+ veterans largely invisible in official counts, with national estimates around 1 million and NY State estimates around 70,000.
chapters.title: Morano on data gaps in VA records for LGBTQ+ veterans

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: At the same time, we have a real problem with data.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: There are major gaps in the data.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Nearly 89% of VA records lack gender identity information, making LGBTQ plus veterans largely invisible in official counts.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: National estimates suggest there are around 1 million LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: While in New York State, there may be around 70,000, most of them believed to be concentrated in New York City, although, as I indicated, because of the lack of data, we don't know exactly for sure.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Together, these realities underscore the importance of ensuring LGBTQ plus veterans are seen, recognized for their service to the country, supported and connected to comprehensive, affirming health care and services that they've earned.

chapters.summary: Morano introduces Intro 914, his bill requiring a study and recommendations on the feasibility of a pilot program for reconsolidation of traumatic memories (RTM) therapy for veterans.
chapters.title: Morano introduces Intro 914 on RTM therapy pilot study

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: In addition to our oversight topic today, we're going to be hearing two very important pieces of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We're going to be hearing intro number 914, which is sponsored by me,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: which is a great bill, as you might expect, which would require a study and recommendations on the feasibility and implementing a pilot program to provide reconsolidation of traumatic memories therapy for veterans.

chapters.summary: Morano explains Intro 914 was originally introduced by former chair Bob Holden, passed committee but never voted on by full council. He expresses openness to amending the bill based on testimony and notes the therapy has been controversial.
chapters.title: Morano on history of Intro 914 and openness to amendments

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let me say a word on this bill.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Unlike any other bill that I've introduced,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This didn't come from an idea that I had or that a constituent brought to me or that I researched.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This bill was introduced by the prior chair of the Veterans Committee, Bob Holden, and even though it had been passed by the Veterans Committee in the last city council, it never got to be voted on by the council as a whole.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So what I'm hoping to get at today is exactly what this particular type of therapy would do.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I know it's been controversial in some quarters and what New York City could do to explore this as a viable therapy for dealing with PTSD.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If this bill needs to be amended to better reflect the realities of how New York City would handle this, I'm all about amending it.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Even though this is my bill, this is not a bill that I have necessarily a rooting interest seeing in its current form.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I am all about changing it or adapting it or expanding it based on the testimony we're going to hear from the administration and hopefully the public.

chapters.summary: Morano introduces Intro 928, sponsored by Minority Leader David Carr, which would amend the administrative code to establish an LGBTQ liaison within the Department of Veterans Services, and invites Carr to speak.
chapters.title: Morano introduces Intro 928 on LGBTQ liaison at DVS

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The other legislation we're hearing today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Is another very important piece of legislation introduced by, which is intro number 928, sponsored by

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Minority Leader David Carr, which would amend the administrative code of the city of New York in relation to establishing an LGBTQ liaison within the Department of Veterans Services.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: For those of you that are unfamiliar with Minority Leader Carr,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: He's the best council member this body has and certainly the best that island has.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I would love for the minority leader to speak on his bill.

chapters.summary: Carr thanks Morano for his leadership of the Veterans Committee, praises his energy and determination, and discusses the importance of supporting veterans who risked their lives for the nation. He also thanks Commissioner Mata for engagement on Staten Island veteran issues.
chapters.title: Carr thanks Morano and Commissioner Mata, discusses veterans' service

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I appreciate the pan of praise, but I have to start by thanking you and commending you for your leadership of this committee since the start of the term.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: You've shown in these last six months an energy, enthusiasm, and determination to improve the lot of the New York City veteran community that I think is not only commendable, but it's necessary.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I think that we always have to have a focus on those who served, many of whom served in combat, many of whom served and came back after traumatic experiences or physically traumatic injuries.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: These people were willing to give their lives for our nation.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And the least we could do is support them now that they're home and their pursuit of jobs and their pursuit of housing and their pursuit of justice in some cases, in pursuit of a life of happiness that they more than earned with their service.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So thank you, Chair, for all of your advocacy on behalf of this wonderful community.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I also want to thank Commissioner Mata for being here today.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: We had a productive conversation last week about the Staten Island veteran community and I think the New York City community at large.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I look forward to continuing to work with you and your team.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I appreciate you being here to give feedback on these pieces of legislation.

chapters.summary: Carr discusses the history of Don't Ask, Don't Tell, noting that between WWII and DADT's 2011 repeal, an estimated 114,000 service members were discharged based on sexual orientation, and argues everyone should be allowed to serve openly.
chapters.title: Carr on history and impact of DADT policy

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: This September will mark 15 years since Congress repealed the infamous don't ask, don't tell policy.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Which was supposed to allow LGBTQ Americans to serve in the military as long as they did not openly

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Disclose their sexual orientation.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Intended as a so-called compromise, which in order to end discrimination against gay and lesbian and bisexual individuals in the military, in many ways it did quite the opposite, forcing people who were willing to give their lives to serve their country

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: an opportunity to go back into the closet, sadly.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And by some estimates, between World War II and DADT's repeal in 2011, the military discharged as many as 114,000 service members on the basis of their actual or perceived sexual orientation.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And I think the lesson of that period before DADT, the period in which it was in place, and then, of course, the period since its repeal, I think the lesson of each of those points in time is that

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: everyone should be allowed to serve openly in our nation's military, regardless of their sexual orientation or their gender identity.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: If you are willing to put your life on the line for the United States of America, that is a commendable thing.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: It shows the inherent bravery and character of who you are.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: That's what speaks to the kind of person that those individuals are who want to serve in that way, and they should all be given the opportunity to do so freely and openly and genuinely as who they are.

chapters.summary: Carr explains that veterans discharged under DADT received less than honorable discharges, denying them GI education benefits, VA health benefits, burials in VA National Cemeteries, and other essential benefits.
chapters.title: Carr on discharge statuses and lost benefits under DADT

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: The military continues in all cases to issue service members a characterization of service upon their discharge or discharge status.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: And most service members receive an honorable discharge status, which entitles each of them to their VA benefits.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: But those who are discharged under DADT received a less than honorable discharge.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: such as a general discharge under honorable conditions, and other than honorable discharge, a bad conduct discharge, or even in some cases a dishonorable discharge.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: As a result, they cannot access some of the essential benefits awarded to those who have served our country, such as the GI education benefits, VA health benefits, burials in the VA National Cemetery, and many, many more.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: When Congress repealed DADT in 2011, the military established a process for veterans discharged under the policy to remove justifications related to their sexual orientation from their paperwork and upgrade their discharge status to honorable.

chapters.summary: Carr describes the burdensome process for veterans to correct discharge records, requiring assembly of old records, attorney assistance, waits of 18+ months, and costly court appeals, with unclear numbers of NY veterans still impacted.
chapters.title: Carr on burden on veterans to correct discharge records

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Yet that process shamefully placed the burden on individual service members to correct their records.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: After many of them had endured years of discrimination due to discharging dating back years, and decades in some cases.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: To complete this procedure, veterans must assemble a variety of old records.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: In most cases, the help of an attorney is required and typically wait 18 months or more for the board that decides these discharge status changes to upgrade those petitions with their respective military branch of service.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: If they exhaust their administrative remedies, veterans must file an appeal in court for which they must seek the help of a lawyer, often at great cost.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: It is unclear how many thousands of New York veterans are still impacted and still have not received the benefits they are due because they were discharged under Don't Ask, Don't Tell.

chapters.summary: Carr discusses Local Law 4 of 2022 which extended city veteran benefits to LGBTQ-discharged veterans and required DVS to offer discharge upgrade assistance. He explains Intro 928 would codify an LGBTQ liaison, require annual reporting, and help address the data problem by encouraging veteran self-identification.
chapters.title: Carr on Local Law 4, Intro 928 details, and data collection needs

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Four years ago, this council unanimously passed legislation authored by former council member and LGBTQIA Caucus Plus chair Danny Drom.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: to help correct this historic injustice.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Local Law 4 of 2022 extended city veteran benefits to those who were discharged because they were LGBTQ and required the city's Department of Veteran Services to offer discharge upgrade assistance to help obtain federal benefits.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: The legislation I've introduced and being heard today, Intro 928, will help fully realize the goals of Local Law 4,

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: establishing an LGBTQ liaison within the Department of Veteran Services, specifically tasked with conducting

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: outreach to LGBTQ veterans, coordinating the department's assistance with discharge characterization upgrades, and connecting these veterans with programs and services offered by DBS and other relevant agencies.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I understand this will largely, hopefully, codify existing practice, but I think there are some practices and policies that are so good, they absolutely need to be enshrined into law.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: It will also require the department to issue an annual report related to assistance for LGBT veterans, which will hopefully and finally give us some idea of how many veterans still suffer from the effects of the shameful legacy of DADT.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I think the chair was quite right.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: We have a data problem with respect to our veteran community.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: I know the commissioner knows this well, and we need to do more to get veterans to self-identify, both within the LGBTQI plus cohort and beyond.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: Because if we're going to start adequately serving the veteran community, particularly with the swath of benefits that we have to offer, we need to make sure that they are able and feel comfortable to come forward, and that means really engaging in a mission of finding them.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: over the next several years.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: So I appreciate the chair hearing this bill, and I look forward to hearing testimony from the administration.

chapters.summary: Morano acknowledges Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa for her effectiveness and mentorship, and Councilmember Phil Wong for his perfect attendance record and recent district visit, joking about a bagel competition.
chapters.title: Morano acknowledges committee members De La Rosa and Wong

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Minority Leader Carr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Well said.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to acknowledge my colleagues on the committee who are here today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to begin by mentioning Councilmember Carmen De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you very much for being here.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I'll note that while we may come from different boroughs, different political parties, and probably different worldviews, I can't think of somebody that has been more effective as a council member.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And when I joined this body, she was one of the first people that I sought out to learn from about how to do this job well.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I don't mind telling you that several of the ideas that she gave me, not only as chair of this committee, but just as a council member in general, have been very stellar.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So on a personal note, thank you very much, council member.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I also want to acknowledge a man who is really distinguishing himself as one of only two council members that stayed for every minute of the marathon finance committee hearing

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: last week and the only one that wasn't the chair.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And so far, this perfect attendance record of his remains intact, which is a neat trick when there are two committees going on at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I also had the privilege of visiting his district on Friday, and the jury's still out of whose district has better bagels, his or mine.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But that is of...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: the co-chair of the Common Sense Caucus of Queens, Phil Wong.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Morano thanks committee staff including legislative counsel Hannah Cohn, policy analyst Maknur Butte, financial analyst Margaret Barnsley, community associate Sebastian Neme, data analyst Colby Porter, Mohamed Shadid, and Kyla Dash, as well as his own staff.
chapters.title: Morano thanks committee and personal staff

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Now, before I conclude, I'd also like to thank the committee staff.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: A lot of people said after Alejandro left the committee staff, we were going to go downhill.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We showed him.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We're just getting started.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank our legislative counsel, Hannah Cohn.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank our policy analyst, Maknur Butte.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank our financial analyst, Margaret Barnsley.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to thank our community associate, Sebastian Neme, data analyst Colby Porter, Mohamed Shadid, and legislative policy analyst Kyla Dash for their hard work in preparing for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'd also like to thank my staff for their work as we continue to serve our constituents and the city as a whole, particularly my chief of staff, Frank Rapacciolo, and our legislative counsel, Pat Russo.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I look forward to today's hearing and to strengthening support for LGBTQ plus veterans throughout New York City.

chapters.summary: Morano introduces four DVS representatives, and committee counsel administers the oath to the administration witnesses before testimony begins.
chapters.title: DVS witnesses sworn in by committee counsel

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We have four very energetic, very dynamic representatives of DVS to offer their insight on both the legislation we're hearing today and the oversight topic that we're looking into.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I'd like to turn it over to our committee counsel to administer the oath to witnesses from the administration.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth in your testimony before this committee and to respond honestly to council members'questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: As a reminder to all our witnesses, please state your name prior to the testimony for the record.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Yesenia Mata introduces herself, thanks the committee for its partnership, and states that today's hearing is about ensuring LGBTQ+ veterans know they are welcome and that DVS is here to support them.
chapters.title: Commissioner Mata opening remarks on DVS services for LGBTQ+ veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Murano and members of the Committee on Veterans and Councilmember Carr.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: My name is Yesenia Mata, and I am the Commissioner of the New York City Department of Veteran Services.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today regarding support and services for LGBTQ plus veterans in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Chair Murano and members of the committee, thank you for your continued partnership and commitment to the veteran community.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We look forward to continuing to work alongside all of you to connect with and serve veterans across the five boroughs.

chapters.summary: Mata addresses terminology nuances, noting city administrative code references LGBTQ+ populations specifically. She discusses her own service as an Army Reserve captain and the diversity of veterans across race, ethnicity, religion, gender identity, and sexual orientation.
chapters.title: Mata on terminology, diversity, and universal DVS services

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I would like to acknowledge an important nuance regarding today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: The language used throughout city administrative code and reporting requirements specifically references LGBTQ plus populations.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: While we recognize and support the full diversity of New York City's communities, including intersex and asexual individuals, our testimony today aligns with the terminology currently established within the city's legislative framework.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: As someone who continues to serve as a captain in the United States Army Reserve, I understand that military service is not a singular experience.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Our veterans come from every borough, neighborhood, and background.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: They represent every race, ethnicity, religion, gender identity, and sexual orientation.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Every program, service, and resource offered by the Department of Veterans Services is available to all veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Today's hearing is not about creating a separate service.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It is about ensuring LGBTQ plus veterans know they are welcome, that they belong, and that DVS is here to support them.

chapters.summary: Mata states DVS supports the goals of Intro 914A but notes the need for amendments to ensure operational feasibility, and fully supports Intro 928, noting DVS has already designated two LGBTQ liaisons: Dana Parrott and Michael Lopez.
chapters.title: Mata on department's position on Intro 914 and 928

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I will also like to briefly address the legislation before the committee today.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Regarding introduction 914A, the department appreciates the intent of expanding access to effective

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: mental health treatments for veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We support the overall goals of the legislation and look forward to continuing discussions with the Council regarding amendments necessary to ensure the proposal is operationally feasible and aligned with agency responsibilities.

chapters.summary: Mata presents statistics on LGBTQ+ veteran populations, noting approximately 1.9 million LGBTQ+ adults who are veterans nationally including 37,000 transgender veterans, with LGBTQ+ veterans more likely to have served in the post-9/11 era.
chapters.title: Mata on LGBTQ+ veteran population statistics

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Regarding Introduction 928, the Department fully supports the legislation and its goal of strengthening engagement with LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: DVS has already designated two LGBTQ liaisons within the department.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Dana Parrott, an Army veteran, as the LGBTQ liaison since 2024, and Michael Lopez, also an Army veteran, now serves as our LGBTQ plus claims and benefits liaison.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: New York City is home to one of the largest LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: populations in the nation.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Nationally, an estimated 1.9 million LGBTQ plus adults and veterans serve, including approximately 37,000 transgender veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: LGBTQ plus veterans are also most likely to have served during the post 9-11 era than the veteran population overall.

chapters.summary: Mata discusses challenges faced by LGBTQ+ veterans including discrimination, housing instability, barriers to health care, social isolation, and the impact of DADT and transgender service restrictions. She notes 36% of LGBTQ+ veterans avoid VA services due to perceived bias.
chapters.title: Mata on challenges and barriers faced by LGBTQ+ veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Many LGBTQ plus veterans have faced unique challenges through their military services and transition to civilian life, including discrimination,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: housing instability, barriers to health care, and social isolation.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: For some, these challenges were compounded by policies such as don't ask, don't tell, and restrictions on transgender military service.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Between 2000 and 2014, approximately 1,300 transgender individuals were discharged from the military because of their gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: While significant progress has been made, barriers remain.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Many are not immediately visible and can include shame, hesitation, or fear.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Of seeking support.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Nationally, 36% of LGBTQ plus veterans report avoiding VA services due to perceived bias.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: No veteran should ever feel that their identity diminishes their service or affects their ability to access the benefits and support they have earned.

chapters.summary: Mata discusses DVS exploring a certificate of eligibility for LGBTQ+ veterans and highlights the discharge upgrade and legal services program (DULS), noting 38 LGBTQ+ veterans sought legal assistance in FY2026. She mentions partnerships with SAGE Vets and Stonewall Veterans Association.
chapters.title: Mata on certificate of eligibility and discharge upgrade program

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: The city has taken important steps to better support LGBTQ plus community.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: including through the mayor's office of LGBTQ plus IA.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Affairs, DVS is currently exploring the creation of a certificate of eligibility for LGBTQ plus veterans, which would help discharge LGBTQ plus veterans access

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: certain city benefits and services available to veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: One area where DVS has been particularly proud to support LGBTQ plus veterans is through our discharge upgrade and legal services program known as duals during fiscal year 2026.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: 38 LGBTQ plus veterans sought legal assistance through the program, many navigating the lasting impact of discriminatory discharge practices.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We are grateful for our partnerships with the Veteran Advocacy Project and the New York Legal Assistance Group, whose expertise has helped veterans pursue discharge upgrades and access earned benefits.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: While many cases remain pending due to the complexity of the review process, these cases underscore the continued need for targeted outreach, legal support, and culturally competent services.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We also recognize the importance

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: of community partnerships, organizations such as Sage Vets and the Stolen Wall Veteran Association provide trusted spaces, social connection, and culturally competent support for the LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: These partnerships

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: are critical to reaching veterans who may not otherwise engage with traditional systems.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We look forward to continuing our work with the council, community organizations, and LGBTQ plus veterans to ensure that every veteran in New York City

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: feels welcome, supported, and empowered.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I'm happy to answer any questions.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how many veterans have interacted with each LGBTQ liaison over the last 12 months. Mata explains DVS does not ask gender identity questions directly, and Nicole elaborates that liaison Dana attends community events in addition to one-on-one client work.
chapters.title: Morano asks about LGBTQ liaison veteran interactions and numbers

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Happy birthday.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And you should know that Ms.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Orlando, who is seated at the table beside you, she also happened to be at the Vietnam Veteran event on Friday with Councilmember Wong, and she was and is just a terrific...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Spokesperson for your agency and is really rep really represents you well

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I have a number of questions say word to the public and to my colleagues that are good enough to participate in today's hearing I have the

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: unique distinction of being on eight City-Council committees so I have had the opportunity to watch every variety of chair

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: holding hearings.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And there are two things that I think both the public and the council members find somewhat challenging and frustrating, to be honest,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: which is the chair will spend 20, 30, 40 minutes asking questions of whoever's testifying before them.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And all these other council members who all have

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: these schedules, they don't get to ask any questions.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And in many cases, the chair just doesn't want the person to ask their question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I mean, I'm speculating, but the bottom line is it's frustrating.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The other thing that's frustrating is, is you get two, three minutes to ask a question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The administration will try to give a comprehensive, lengthy answer, and your time's up.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: No time for an appropriate follow-up question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I'll just say to my colleagues that are good enough to be here and participate in today's hearing, I'm going to ask a couple of questions, then turn it over to you guys.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You've got five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But if you need more than five minutes, take more than five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If there's an appropriate follow-up question, ask it.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Ask it.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We're not going to kick anybody out and grab the hook after five minutes.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: But trust me, I have plenty of questions on my own.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let me begin, Commissioner, with your testimony regarding the LGBTQ plus liaisons.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How many veterans, you indicated that there's already two LGBTQ plus liaisons.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How many veterans have interacted with each liaison over the last 12 months?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: DBS, we necessarily don't ask the gender identity question.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: DBS, when a veteran comes in, we ask for the type of services that they need as

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: DBS, we want to make sure that it does create a welcoming space for every individual to feel comfortable at DBS.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Oftentimes, the individual, him or herself, decides to disclose the type of service that they need or the type of gender identity that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: that they would like to identify as.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, at DBS, we don't actually ask that question directly.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, with the gender liaison that we do have in place and is in 2024,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: that specific liaison has provided support to individuals by helping them connect with partners that can go further on providing the services that they need.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But most recently, we added a second LGBTQ plus liaison, and the goal is to work closely with the LGBTQ plus committee and the LGBTQIA plus

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: mayor's office to see.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: How can we do better at DVS to provide support to the LGBTQ plus IA community?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I'll let Nicole further elaborate on that.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Our current LGBTQ plus liaison, Donna, she has also attended different community events in the past as well.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So in addition to just being one-on-one on the client side of it, she's also participating in the community in different events that pop up, specifically relating to LGBTQ plus issues as well.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And we can follow up with the listing.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how DVS measures the effectiveness of existing liaisons. Mata says DVS is working on better intake processes to capture gender identity and demographic data, noting the lack of such data is appalling.
chapters.title: Morano asks about measuring liaison effectiveness

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And how do we measure?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: the effectiveness of those existing liaisons.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: At the moment, that's what we're working at at DBS.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I am really looking forward to working with Councilmember Carr, with the LGBTQ plus committee, and the mayor's office,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: LGBTQIA plus office, and also with various other nonprofits to help us understand how can we measure that success.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, the census or the VA don't have that data questionnaire or

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: intake that can help us establish where, like the accurate number of LGBTQIA veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And that, for my end, that's appalling.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So for DVS this year, what we're trying to do is to ensure that we can actually have a better intake process of not just understanding the, let's just say, the veteran status, but also gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Also, what is your background?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Are you, for example, Latino?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What type of Latino background?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What type of, in general, we want to...

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Really understand where veterans, where they are coming from and the need that they really, that there are needs.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So when I do, when you, to answer that question is that we're working better in our intake process.

chapters.summary: Morano asks what operational changes Intro 928 would create given DVS already has two liaisons. Mata and Nicole explain the bill would add reporting requirements beyond just discharge upgrades, capturing broader community needs and outreach data.
chapters.title: Morano asks what Intro 928 would change operationally

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If DVS already has two designated liaisons, what operational changes would Intro 928, Minority Leader Carr's bill, actually create?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What would be different?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Well, first of all, I want to thank Councilmember Carr for that intro because it really pushes an agency even further to provide that support to LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Even though we had one liaison, now we have two.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And having the second liaison.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: At first, when it came to DVS, we will focus primarily on the upgrade discharge, which Nicole, my colleague, will elaborate further.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But now.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Now, with the second liaison, we will go even further on upgrading our intake process.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, the VA or the census itself doesn't ask that question about LGBTQ+.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So the goal is through this second liaison to work in conjunction with Councilmember Carr and the LGBTQ plus committee and the LGBTQ plus I.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: LGBTQIA plus mayor's office to ensure that we can together develop an intake process where individuals feel comfortable of disclosing that information.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Also, operationally, what would change is the reporting requirements on this because currently the only we report on local law for which is in regards to discharge upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: But we know and we respect that this community reaches out to us separate of just discharge upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So by being able to take what our liaisons are doing and translate it into data and into reporting, that gives us a better picture of what the needs of the community are in addition to discharge upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We're really looking forward to implementing that piece of it.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how many outreach events specifically targeted LGBTQ+ veterans were conducted. Nicole reports three targeted events in the last fiscal year plus monthly Veterans Mental Health Coalition meetings covering LGBTQ+ topics.
chapters.title: Morano asks about LGBTQ-targeted outreach events

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Are you able to tell us how many outreach events specifically targeted LGBTQ plus veterans and were conducted by these liaisons either last year or the last fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So I'm going to pass this question to Nicole.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So as more recently, I am taking a closer look at the type of

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: of events that we have been doing across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What's different this time around is that we are being very intentional as to if we go to a specific borough, what type of service are we, who are we teaming up with, why are we teaming up with them, and the type of services that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: the individual, the veteran will get if they decide to go to this specific nonprofit.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So I just want to discuss what we're doing internally right now.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Internally, I am updating our partners that we have.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Even our partners have to go through an intake process.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Soon we'll need to go through an intake process again.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: If we're going to be partnering up with individuals, I want to make sure that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: With nonprofits, we want to make sure that veterans themselves do get the support that they need when they do go to these great partners that we have.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: In the last fiscal year, we had three events that were specifically targeted for LGBTQ plus veterans, but then every month there is a Veterans Mental Health Coalition meeting that includes topics on LGBTQ plus support.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Please.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You didn't expect me to go to you so quickly.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about veterans reluctant to identify as LGBTQ+ due to past discrimination. Mata discusses the need for awareness campaigns led by directly impacted individuals and the challenge of self-identification, noting DVS lacks specific data.
chapters.title: Wong asks about data collection and reluctant LGBTQ veterans

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I'm concerned about your data collection regarding LGBTQ veterans'populations.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, you have been doing outreach, but...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Have you encountered veterans that may be reluctant to identify themselves?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: As LGBTQ plus do the past discrimination or they feel uncomfortable seeking service?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How do you handle cases like that?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Veterans themselves have a hard time to self-identify to begin with, and that is why as the SDBS, we do want to do a campaign collectively, again, with the support of various other nonprofits.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But even further, right, when it comes to LGBTQ plus veterans, that is another layer of work that we have to do, a campaign awareness.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, this requires to have individuals that are directly impacted to help us lead this campaign.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But we understand that when it does come to data, that DVS doesn't have that specific data in hand.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And we do hope that with this second liaison that we can, again, create this intake process that we can work collaboratively with various other nonprofits to do this.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What we do have is the data of the duals upgrade discharge that supports the veterans that were discharged due to their gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I'll let Nicole elaborate on that.

chapters.summary: Wong asks how DVS plans to find LGBTQ veterans for the certificate of eligibility if identification is difficult. Nicole explains the certificate would serve as a safe space where veterans can verify service without showing DD-214s that reveal DADT discharges.
chapters.title: Wong asks about certificate of eligibility identification approach

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I want to back, I'm reading your statement.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You're saying that you're going to explore the creation of a certificate of eligibility of LGBTQ.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So what is your approach if you have problems identifying them?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Then how do you find these LGBTQ veterans and give them the certificate of eligibility?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so to speak a little bit more on their certificate.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So what we've been noticing is that on the state level and the city level, there are veterans who are discharged due to their status as LGBTQ, whether that was under Don't Ask, Don't Tell, the trans ban, or anything else.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And for us, what we're seeing is that there's a lot of fear in applying for benefits due to the fear that you have to show your DD-214 that says you were discharged under Don't Ask, Don't Tell, or whatever it may be.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So for us, what we're acting as is that safe space for them to come to us and say, I've served my country,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: show us their paperwork in a trusted space with our LGBT plus liaison, who will then be able to verify their service,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: create a certificate that then can be given to different city agencies to verify that service so that they don't actually have to provide their DD-214

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: that says what they were discharged for while they may be in the process of upgrading or whatever that may be, but still verifies their military service.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So for us, we don't want someone's fear of having to show their discharge be a barrier to getting a benefit.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that's why we want to be those that will provide that certificate that won't say anything on it.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: It will just verify their service.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about outreach to health clinics like those on Flushing Avenue. Mata and Noah Bray explain DVS connects veterans to VA, NYC Health and Hospitals, Pride Health Centers, and Northwell, emphasizing the importance of building rapport through claims agents.
chapters.title: Wong asks about outreach to health clinics and Vet Centers

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Veterans, like, visit health clinics, like the ones in Flushing Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is there any outreach?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Have you done with them to identify these veterans that require health services?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about that?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I'm going shortly going to pass this question to Donnie and Noah, but

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: at DVS, part of the claims team, when someone does come to DVS, oftentimes veterans go through the claims process first as they are in need, whether different types of benefits.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And when they do disclose that they need any sort of health care support, we do connect them to the VA, but also we do connect them to NYC Health and Hospitals,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Pride Health Centers, and Northwell as they provide LGBTQIA plus fertility services.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, this is if the individual does disclose that they need LGBTQ plus services.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, I'm going to now pass it on to Noah that can further elaborate on the claims portion when it comes to the health care component.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Thanks, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: I'm Noah Bray.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: I'm the executive director of the support services for DDS.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think that conversation that you mentioned, the difficulty just getting them to disclose a veteran status, it does go further to disclose anything, even like a name and an address.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So I think it's a testament to the claims agents is just to create that rapport and make that veteran comfortable just having a conversation.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: With that comes everything else, like any kind of like medical conditions or anything that has to do with that claim that we're going to file.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So that's where we get that information.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Once we develop that relationship, it allows us to connect them to whatever benefits or whatever partnerships that we have.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And that's where that magic happens.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So that's where we overcome that hurdle.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Please feel free to continue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, just a follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Like, we have a Queens Vet Center right at the border of my district that I see veterans go there all the time requesting medical services.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering that the outreach that you have done, say, with them or other vet centers, that you may be able to identify or help them identify LGBT veterans.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's an issue that's always in my mind when every time I drive past them, like, is DVS outreaching?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: To these veterans because they go there a lot.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We do find that partnership with that vet center as well as all the other vet centers and the VAs.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Those are part of our regular circuit that we try to send our claims agents to.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So we do have usually a liaison for each one of those entities.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: But it does happen organically.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We leave nobody out for partnership opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So whether it's a vet center or a nonprofit, we try to send our claims agents out as part of the outreach.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And we've had some pretty good success so far.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I just wanted to add to that.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: One of the unique aspects I had.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: is that when it comes to claims and housing, one of the major needs for veterans, it is led by veterans themselves.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Noah is a Coast Guard veteran and Dinesh is also an Army vet.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So it also provides us that extra cultural competency, per se, to be able to provide that support to veterans.

chapters.summary: Wong asks if DVS needs help connecting to LGBTQ nonprofits. Mata welcomes the support, discusses staff training on organizing with LGBTQ+ community, and mentions revamping partner intake processes.
chapters.title: Wong asks about connecting to LGBTQ nonprofits

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Do you need assistance in reaching out to nonprofits that work with LGBT individuals and then work with them to identify veterans?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is there something you are currently doing or you might need help in connecting to these nonprofits?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We welcome any support.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Myself, as my background as an organizer, myself, that is one of the major components that we are issuing at DVS, providing training to the staff as well.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And it's just not training about services, but training when it comes to how do you organize with the LGBTQ plus community.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But in general, when it comes to LGBTQ plus support, it keeps evolving as time goes.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So that is one of the major components that we are making sure that the staff knows how to work with the LGBTQ plus community, but also how do we revamp our partnerships that we have.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: As I said before, I am revamping the list that we have that would entail an

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: take for each of our partners to see what type of services that they provide.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But as always, we want to make sure that we have LGBTQ plus veterans on the table so they could help us organize and be able to reach out to individuals in different boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And we'd love to work with your office as well.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I'll have maybe around two questions, but please.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about the 38 LGBTQ+ veterans who sought discharge upgrade assistance in FY26. Nicole reports about 20 cases remain pending and 8 discharge upgrades were completed in the last two fiscal years.
chapters.title: Morano asks about discharge upgrade cases pending and successful

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Just going back to your testimony for a moment, and then I have a couple of other things following up on Councilmember Wong's questions.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You stated that 38 LGBTQ plus veterans sought assistance through the discharge upgrade program in fiscal year 26.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Of those 38 veterans, how many cases remain pending?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So, yes, in fiscal year 26, we have had 38 LGBTQ plus veterans work with our partners, whether that be at VAP or NILAG for discharge upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Currently, what we are seeing is that there are about 20 that are still pending.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That for us can mean a range of things, whether information is still being gathered, whether it's further along in the process.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: But that is something we can follow up more on the intricacies with.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Do we know at this point how many resulted in successful discharge upgrades?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So in the last fiscal, I think in the last two fiscal years, we have seen eight discharge upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How many veterans, maybe the answer is the same, regained VA benefits as a result of that?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That would be a little bit different just because there's the difference between a discharge upgrade being totally changed versus the VA characterization of upgrade, which means that you're still then entitled to VA benefits even if your discharge hasn't fully been updated.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about average case duration and barriers. Nicole explains cases take one to three years, with barriers including scattered records, agency reluctance to share information, and trauma requiring mental health support during the process.
chapters.title: Morano asks about case duration and barriers to discharge upgrades

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: For my edification, how long does the average discharge upgrade case take?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: It could take from one to three years.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And there's a little bit of a delay, whether that be in the actual courts themselves, in information gathering, sometimes in clients falling off and then getting reengaged, but about one to three years.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What do you think the biggest barriers are to...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: to obtaining upgrades?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think that it has to do with the information gathering, where there's records all over the place.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: In some cases, some agencies are less reluctant to give out this information that is needed.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: If someone has been stationed at different areas across the country, it's coordinating with those.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We even see this on the...

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: For a disability claim issues with getting those health records.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So it's about getting everything in the same place, as well as recognizing there's a lot of trauma and mental health support that is needed when dealing with these cases.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how many potentially eligible veterans remain unaware of the discharge upgrade program. Mata and Nicole discuss the need for better awareness campaigns and note an anticipated increase due to the trans ban, with targeted messaging being developed.
chapters.title: Morano asks about veterans unaware of discharge upgrade program

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How many potentially eligible veterans do you believe might remain unaware of the program?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: The program itself, so at DVS, we do want to do a campaign awareness of the services, one that we provide, but also what the duals program is.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Oftentimes, if you are a veteran or have served in the military, you understand the, I would like to say the language, right?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, oftentimes when it comes to family members of those that serve, they don't realize that this is a type of service that exists even for their veteran themselves.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So at DBS, we do have to do a better campaign awareness to showcase one of the services that we provide, but also when it comes to the duals program.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But even at that, the duals program itself.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It does take time, as Nicole mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It takes one to three years to do this type of support, and we would like support from this council to highlight the work that DBS is doing, to highlight the work when it comes to the duals program.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think in addition to that as well is that there's also people who file for discharge upgrades separate of just a reason being whether it's don't ask, don't tell or whatever that may be.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: What we do anticipate for, though, is an increase as more people are being affected by the trans ban.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And we're seeing that being more prevalent.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that's something we're actively tracking with our partners at VAP.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: To ensure we're doing more targeted messaging for those affected by that ban as well.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about the certificate of eligibility. Nicole explains it would cover four city benefits requiring DD-214 presentation, allowing veterans to present a certificate instead of a discharge paperwork showing DADT-related status.
chapters.title: Morano asks about certificate of eligibility details and benefits

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: One thing that caught our ear was the certificate of eligibility, because it was new, at least to me.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What exactly would this certificate do?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So this certificate would be able to, there's about four different city benefits that are executed on a city level that someone needs to provide their DD-214 for.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So they may still be able to receive the benefit even though they are dishonorably discharged, but there's a lot of shame in presenting that when you do have, you know, you're a dishonorable discharge for under don't ask, don't tell.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So what we're doing on the city level is for those benefits where that DD-214 needs to be presented, instead of them having to present that DD-214, they would

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: present the certificate that would be given out by our agency where information about service would be verified from our agency so that they don't need to provide that to the different city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Do you know which four benefits that that would unlock?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so that would unlock different residency points for civil service exams through DCAS.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: There's NICER's pension buyback.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: There's Department of Finance for tax benefits.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And I believe there's two different types for Department of Finance.

chapters.summary: Morano asks if legislation is needed for the certificate and how eligibility is determined. Nicole confirms it falls under DVS's charter authority, requires no new legislation, and would be executed by the LGBTQ+ claims liaison with a target of mid-next year for implementation.
chapters.title: Morano asks if legislation needed and about eligibility determination timeline

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Do we think this would require legislation, or is that something that the agency could do on their own?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So this is something that's actually in our charter to be able to do.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So this is something that we are now acting on.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: out from the charter.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: This is also something that is required by the state to do and they execute it as a letter.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we'll be executing it as a certificate along with a letter.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How would eligibility be determined?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so the individual cases would go to our LGBTQ plus claims liaison who would take account of all the different service paperwork, who will even be able to verify the DD-214,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: who has experience dealing with cases like these as well as being military culturally competent, and then they will be executing the certificate.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And is this being developed specifically for LGBTQ plus veterans or for LGBTQ plus New Yorkers generally?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: This is specifically for LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Do you guys have a timeline that you envision for implementation?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so this is something that we're in active conversations with the mayor's office of LGBTQ plus IA affairs, as well as we have now identified the benefits and are connecting with the agencies who will be receiving these certificates.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we are hoping by mid next year to be able to actually execute probably before, but we want to ensure that we're having the correct conversation so that there's no barriers in using this.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how DVS has implemented Local Law 4 of 2022, which requires DVS to assist LGBTQ+ veterans seeking discharge upgrades. Nicole describes partnerships with VAP and NILAG, timely referrals, and plans for more direct outreach to contractors.
chapters.title: Morano asks about Local Law 4 implementation

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You brought up, Ms.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Orlando, Local Law 4.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Local Law 4, for people that may not know, requires DVS to assist LGBTQ plus veterans seeking discharge characterization upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Specifically, how has DVS,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: implemented this law?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
