utterances.text: So we maintain very strong partnerships with VAP and with NILAG to ensure that the veterans that we're referring over that may come to our office are getting

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: connected appropriately in a timely fashion, are getting access to that support, as well as for us making sure that we're continuing to promote the services, to your point, for people that may not know that that is an option,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: as well as we're looking to explore in the future how we can do more outreach and engagement directly with the contractors to ensure that people are aware of the services.

chapters.summary: Morano asks why male veterans contacted DVS for discharge upgrade assistance at higher rates than other genders. Mata attributes this to self-identification challenges and the need for better awareness campaigns, especially for women and LGBTQ+ veterans.
chapters.title: Morano asks about Local Law 4 data trends by gender

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Local, in terms of some of the trends that we've seen with Local Law 4 of 2022,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: after comparing data from all available reports on Local Law 4, we see that between fiscal year 2022 and 2025,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: among male, female, and non-binary veterans, veterans identifying as male contacted DVS most frequently for discharge upgrade assistance.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Is there a reason male veterans contacted DVS at a higher rate rather than the rest of the population?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, it goes back to self-identification.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: The first hearing that you did, which we're so grateful, was on women veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Well, and I'm grateful as a woman veteran yourself for your participation in that.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: When it comes to that, we have...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Along with Ms.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Smalls, I would say.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I do want to also highlight that Ms.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Smalls was also inducted in the Hall of Fame.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Oh.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But I think it's like, for example, the perfect example here, right?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We need to make sure that we can bring that awareness that there's women veterans that serve that we have different types of backgrounds.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I think it goes back to the LGBTQ plus component.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We need to do a better campaign awareness of that.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: There's LGBTQ plus veterans that are currently serving and that they do a magnificent job.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So, again, it goes back to the campaign awareness and women are serving at a higher rate as well.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And we need to highlight those ones, too.

chapters.summary: Morano asks why Navy veterans contacted DVS for discharge upgrades at higher rates. Nicole hypothesizes it relates to outreach targeting but says DVS is looking at how different tactics might affect the numbers.
chapters.title: Morano asks about Navy veterans trend in discharge upgrades

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: The report also show that veterans from the Navy contacted DVS for discharge upgrade assistance at a higher rate than any other branch of the military.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Is there a reason for this trend?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: While we don't know exactly the reason why that may be, we do think that it does have to do with outreach and with the groups that are being targeted in a lot of this outreach.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And that is our hypothesis right now.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: But we're looking forward to seeing how different tactics would affect that number.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I noticed you guys wore navy white in tribute to the high participation rates of naval veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I wore...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Coast Guard blue in recognition of Noah's service and Ms.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Orlando's service with the Auxiliary Coast Guard.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about the FY25 spike in discharge upgrade seekers. Nicole reports no similar trend in FY26 but notes increased need compared to FY22, attributing the spike to better outreach, stronger partnerships, and the federal landscape including the trans ban.
chapters.title: Morano asks about FY25 increase in discharge upgrades

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: In fiscal year 25, the number of veterans seeking discharge upgrades drastically increased among all genders and military branches.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What changed?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Are you seeing a similar trend this year?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Currently for fiscal year 26, we did not see a similar trend.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: What we do see is that there's still that increasing need compared to fiscal year 22, where we only had two people.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So for us, we think that what contributed to that spike was different outreach efforts, awareness of the services, stronger partnerships that we are having with our legal service providers.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And as we are continuing to build that capacity as well, we anticipate that for that to increase.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We also believe that in this landscape that we're in with the trans van being introduced in January of 2025, there's a lot of

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: stigma and a lot of discourse going on in the community where people may be more than not likely to actually identify and to actually come out for those benefits and supports.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we do think the federal landscape has some effect on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And that's why we wanted to highlight the important need of, and I think it goes back to what you mentioned about how many people know about the dual

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: discharge program that is in place at the moment, because that will also give.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: that support system that they need, but also feel welcome and feel that, hey, there's support here for us.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So that is something that we do want to do better and highlight that program that we have in place.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about differences in veterans discharged pre vs. post DADT repeal seeking upgrades. Nicole says they are tracking this anecdotally, seeing veterans come in due to the trans ban, and are preparing for increased engagement.
chapters.title: Morano asks about pre/post DADT repeal discharge upgrade differences

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I noticed you alluded to the trans ban earlier.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Have you observed a difference in the number of veterans who were discharged pre and post the repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell seeking discharge characterization upgrades?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: This is something that we have been seeing anecdotally.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we are still working on what those exact numbers look like, but we have had veterans come to us because of the trans ban, and we're working on seeing what support we can provide.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: In some cases, it may necessarily not just be the discharge upgrade and other issues that have came up, but this is something that we are actively tracking.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: As we think on the Don't Ask, Don't Tell side, we're monitoring the lifelong effects of that, even from when someone gets their discharge upgrade, but we're anticipating and preparing for more of those that will be engaging with us because of the trans ban.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about transgender discharges between 2000-2014 and how DVS relays eligibility messages. Nicole and Noah explain the current trans ban discharges for gender dysphoria diagnosis and the challenge of reaching back out to veterans who were discharged for their identity.
chapters.title: Wong asks about transgender discharges and outreach challenges

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Jim.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I want to go back because.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I'm a little confused over here.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So you have...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Between 2000 and 2014, approximately 1,300 transgender individuals are discharged from the military because of their gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's because it's a direct result of don't ask, don't tell, right?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Or this is because of something else.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I think my question is, you have to go back to them and saying that you are eligible for benefits.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And how are you going to relay that message?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because it seemed to me that...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: They got discharged because of their gender identity, and now you want to go back and say, oh, we want you to come forward because of your gender identity.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You're allowed these benefits.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It seems to be a message that they don't even want to understand.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so in regards to transgender individuals who were discharged for that prior to Don't Ask, Don't Tell, that is something that we are seeing is happening even more now with there's directly a trans ban.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So currently right now, if you have a diagnosis of gender dysphoria, you are unable to serve in the military and you will be discharged.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So for us, what we're trying to see is that there are certain city benefits, even if you may not be eligible for federal benefits, and I'll pass over to Noah to speak a little bit more,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: but that you are here in New York City able to be eligible for the benefits that you have served, and we can provide that documentation to show that.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Yes, it's another layer that we have to deal with to find those veterans.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Like you said, now we have to go back and let them know, like, you are eligible for more benefits than you think.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And, you know, for the veteran community,

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: It's tough just to find them to say, hey, you're eligible for just the general, the most general benefits.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: But it is just a little step further.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: It's a little bit harder to find them, those that were discharged necessarily, and say, please come back to the, you know, back into the fold.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We want to help you.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We want to help you get your discharge changed or get you eligible for benefits.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So it goes back to that outreach component.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So just starting that conversation about just benefits in general.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And then we from there, then we get to the ideally those discharge upgrade cases.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about surviving spouses of LGBTQ veterans' eligibility for benefits and outreach. Noah Bray says they are creating subject matter experts for surviving spouse claims and that eligibility is typically yes but very nuanced based on individual circumstances.
chapters.title: Wong asks about surviving spouses of LGBTQ veterans' benefits

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have a follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You have a...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: For surviving spouses of LGBTQ veterans, are they eligible for benefits?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And how are you going to outreach to this particular population?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We do have two claims benefits that we are creating like a subject matter expert in that realm and the surviving spouse realm.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So that would fall under their purview.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So it's a similar thing where we send them out into the communities or with the nonprofit organizations that tend to have a higher population of potential surviving spouses.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And then from the beginnings of those conversations, it's very similar in that once we have that general benefit conversation, it will lead.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Again, that's the magic, and it speaks to the claims agent's abilities to be able to connect with those veterans or their surviving spouses to get them to those benefits.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about proving marriage relationships for surviving spouses when paperwork may not exist. Noah confirms this is a challenge and that cases are very individualistic depending on factors like length of cohabitation and age. Nicole notes DVS is not aware of any surviving spouses denied benefits.
chapters.title: Wong asks about proving marriage relationships for surviving spouses

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But are they eligible for the same benefits as other surviving spouses?

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Typically, yes.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: But very nuanced.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Everything's individualistic depending on sometimes as much as age of the veteran or when they died, things like that.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: But typically, yes.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And if I could just jump in right now, our agency is currently not aware of any surviving spouses that were denied benefits.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You do need to provide marriage type of relationships to prove that you're a surviving spouse.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because a lot of these relationships, they may not even be paperwork that may back this up.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And that's what goes back into the factors of like on the individual case, because there's so many things that are involved with like the length of time they live together or the age of, let's say, if you're looking for education benefits for the children alive, it's very case specific.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Joe.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how many veterans DVS serves self-identify as LGBTQ+. Nicole confirms DVS does not currently collect that information in intake but is exploring updates. Mata emphasizes the importance of holistic data collection.
chapters.title: Morano asks about data collection gaps for LGBTQ veterans

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to go back to data collection because I think it's clear, based on what everybody said, that the data collection gap is

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: the biggest blind spot for how to best serve the LGBTQ plus veteran community.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: How many veterans served by DVS?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: self-identify as LGBTQ+.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that is not something that we include in our intake for that self-identification question.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That's what we're exploring on updating.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: But currently, we do not take that information.

chapters.summary: Mata discusses DVS targets for increasing engagement, emphasizing the need to understand veterans holistically beyond just military status. She wants to develop intake processes that capture gender identity and background with input from LGBTQ+ veteran advocates.
chapters.title: Discussion on DVS targets for engagement and intake process improvements

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Does DVS have a target for increasing engagement with LGBTQ plus veterans?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: As Nicole mentioned, that is something that DVS in the past has not collected.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, the type of environment DVS wants to create or is creating is to ensure that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Veterans feel welcome at DBS and that they get the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, veterans come for different types of services.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, as the new commissioner, I understand the importance of data, the importance of understanding the veteran holistically other than just a veteran or only just to keep in,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: only just put the veteran in one box of just thinking of them as military.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We understand that they come from different backgrounds.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Even, for example, we belong to different boroughs, and even I prefer getting my services as a Staten Islander.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I also am a bit different than people that are in Brooklyn and Queens or Bronx, right?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It's just based on the location that you live in.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So at DVS, I do want to do an intake process or intake documentation where.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We not only understand that they're veterans, but that we know, like, for example,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What's their type of gender identity?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Also, their background.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It's learning about the veteran holistically.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, if we are going to do an intake and provide that question, I do want to ensure that it comes from advocates themselves and that we have them at the table and that they...

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Tell us how the question should be in the intake.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So it's going to take, I do want to ensure that we can have the LGBTQ plus veterans at the table so they could help us understand how can the LGBTQ plus veteran community can better self-identify.

chapters.summary: Morano asks what data DVS wishes it had but doesn't collect. Nicole identifies demographic data and the unemployment rate for NYC veterans as key missing data points, noting even BLS only tracks this at the state level.
chapters.title: Morano asks what data DVS wishes it had but doesn't collect

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: This might be self-evident based on what,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Both of you just said, but what data does DVS wish that it had right now, but currently doesn't collect?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Is it the self-identification question or is it something else?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think if we're speaking also more generally, I think the demographic data would be super helpful because we know that there's so many services along the city that can be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That may not be veteran specific, but can be used by a veteran.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think also just speaking more in general, for me, something that has been a data point that would be super helpful, and this is totally separate than this, is the unemployment rate for veterans in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That has been a data point that is impossible for anyone to find.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: BLS does not even hold that data.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: It's only on a state level.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That would be key for a lot of connection to these different services.

chapters.summary: Mata and Nicole discuss Local Law 37 data collection efforts, including recommendations to improve questionnaire language and working with larger agencies to execute MOUs for timely data sharing across city agencies.
chapters.title: Discussion on Local Law 37 data collection across city agencies

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I know that we are currently working with

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: other agencies when it comes to Local Law 37 to ensure that the questionnaire about veterans is

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: inputted in their application process because then with that it will help us understand where veterans are going.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: To get the services that they need, but also what types of services they're requesting the most.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And Nicole can elaborate the efforts and the wins that we are having on ensuring that agencies can join us on that.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So specifically for Local Authority 7, and thank you, Chair, for collaborating with us on this, we have made some recommendations in terms of the language to better reflect what our report would need to capture.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we're just finalizing with our friends over at City Hall, and then we'll be sending them back over just for further conversations, as well as we're currently working with the

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: larger agencies to ensure that this is something that will be doable and making sure that they are able to provide this data in a timely matter, as well as we're working to execute those MOUs so that we are able to have a better way to ensure we're getting this data and that it's coming in a timely fashion.

chapters.summary: Morano asks how DVS knows whether outreach efforts are succeeding. Mata discusses upgrading referral tracking systems and developing case management to follow outcomes. Nicole notes increased engagement through social media, newsletters, roundtables, and VAB meetings.
chapters.title: Morano asks about measuring outreach success

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: One of the things I struggle with as a council member, as chair of this committee, as somebody that plays a leading role in some other entities within the city council,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: is wondering what the best way to reach people is and whether my outreach efforts are successful.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Sometimes it's easy to see if you see 9,000 people commenting on a social media post.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It's really...

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: reaching somebody, but there's all sorts of people that aren't on social media.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: How does DVS know whether outreach efforts are succeeding, whatever the outreach efforts are, whether they're related to LGBTQ plus veterans or some other veteran cohort?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We are seeing a DVS, for example, from last year to this year, specifically from February and on, that there has been an increase on

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: data in the sense of who we are outreaching, how we have referred individuals.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, what we are trying to do is upgrade our systems when it does come to, for example, if we are referring individuals at DVS, we are upgrading that system.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What does it mean once that individual is referred?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What was the outcome with that specific individual?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: In a sense, we also want to have like a case management system in place to be able to support the veteran in the long term, not just a one try.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I'll let Nicole elaborate further on that too.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think to your point, it's easy to see more in the numbers, right?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: People are subscribing to the newsletter.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We're getting views on social media.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think for us, what that has been translating to is more people being engaged with us.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So in our series of roundtable, we're seeing more engagement directly from advocates.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: In our VAB meetings, we're seeing the high.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: number of participants than we've seen.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So I think for us, it's leveraging and really playing around with social media and with our newsletter, because that has been translating to more people showing up.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And also with the partnerships that we are doing, again, they're very intentional, the partnerships and the events that we are doing at DBS.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: For example, we're doing an event.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We need to understand why we're doing that event.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: How many people we have outreached at that event, how many people attended that event, how many people did the intake process.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: For example, I'm developing that intake form that I want to, but again, I want to work with various other veteran groups so they could help us finalize that intake form.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But also is like once they're referred, again, like we're following, we're making sure that there's

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: that there's a follow-through with that referral.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But what we are seeing is that a lot of the partnerships have already existed at DBS is that we just needed to revamp those partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Speaking of partnerships, I know you alluded to a few of them, including the Veteran Advocacy Project and the Stonewall Veterans Association.

chapters.summary: Morano asks which partnerships generate the highest referrals. Nicole identifies SAGE as the primary referral source for LGBTQ+ veterans, and notes VA and city agencies like the Office to End Gender-Based Violence and Department of Finance for general referrals.
chapters.title: Morano asks about partnerships generating most referrals

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Which partnerships generate the highest number of referrals?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: In terms for LGBTQ plus veterans specifically or partnerships in general?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Well, I mean, I'll take both.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, I think for LGBTQ plus veterans in general, SAGE has been a primarily referral source for us as they participate in our mental health roundtables, in our veteran mental health coalition meetings, as well as at different outreach events that we have in the community.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So I would say SAGE is our primary.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And then for partnerships in general, working very closely with our federal partners, make a lot of referrals over to the VA, but as well as to our partners at different city agencies, whether that be the Office to End Gender-Based Violence

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: or Department of Finance for anything taxation-related.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So right now what we're working on is, like Commissioner said, being more intentional about where we are sending people over, more specifically locally, so targeting some of those smaller groups.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Obviously, I know at least a few of us are from Staten Island.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about borough-level partnership network differences. Mata discusses the uniqueness of each borough and the need to better reach non-veteran-designated nonprofits. Nicole highlights staff borough representation as key to forging local relationships.
chapters.title: Morano asks about borough partnership networks

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Are there boroughs where partnership networks are stronger than others?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Obviously, you alluded to the cultural differences of being a veteran and seeking assistance or seeking programs in a place like Staten Island versus Brooklyn or Queens.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Well, we do know Staten Island is a veteran-friendly borough and does provide a welcoming atmosphere to many veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What we are seeing is that every borough is unique and does provide, like the services are there.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: There's so many services available, one to veterans, but also many nonprofits that perhaps feel that because they're not veteran designated that they should not support veterans per se or may not have the cultural competency.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So again, every borough does provide that support and services to veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: However, I do want to bring it back to DVS that we do need to do a better job on.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: On reaching out to those nonprofits that perhaps have never been involved with veterans but can and further highlight the services that are available to veterans in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So that's going to be an ongoing effort.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But again, when it comes to being a welcoming borough for veterans, I do know that Staten Island is one of them.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But our goal is to do better,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: provide better services to veterans by showing them what, if they go to, for example, Brooklyn or Queens, like what kind of service there is available for them there.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And in general, every borough.

chapters.summary: Donnie Smalls highlights strong housing partnerships in Queens and the Bronx, and work with Councilmember Yusef-Salaam in Manhattan. Noah Bray praises the Vallone Initiative, library community, VFW, and American Legion as key claims-side partners across boroughs.
chapters.title: Smalls and Bray on borough-specific partnerships and outreach

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: A few questions.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Please, do you want to add something?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, if I just can add to that, I think we've also been super intentional about our staff because our staff,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We know at the end of the day, it's those who are in the boroughs who are going to be able to forge those relationships.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: When I started as an intern six years ago, I was the only one in the office from Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we're really excited to see that that has been growing as well as we have many different staff members from now showing every single borough.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that's something that we've been really empowering to to our staff members to be able to represent their borough and really have those connections locally.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We're taking over the city.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I'm going to have Donnie and Noah elaborate also what they're doing in each borough and their staff in each borough is doing.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Morning, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Donnie Smalls, Associate Commissioner for the Department of Veteran Services.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: So the housing team.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: And Hall of Famer.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: The housing team go out to all boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Their boroughs, Queens and the Bronx, are very strong.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Both borough presidents are very strong on a veteran housing aspect and veteran issues across the board.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I wanted to point that out, but it's a lot of Manhattan is getting there.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: It's getting there as far as the support that's needed.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Councilmember Yusef-Salaam, I've worked with him to help self-identify veterans in his district, which I live in.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: So I'm in very close partnership with him and his staff to help identify veterans in the Manhattan area.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: as well.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Noah?

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Hall of Fame status, please, Noah.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: What's that?

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Hall of Fame status.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Pending, aspiring.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Gotcha.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We, I have to say the Valone Initiative has been great for us too when you talk about referrals and just partnerships in general because they are, as you know, everywhere in all the boroughs and all the nooks and crannies with us.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So they've been a fantastic partner for us.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Also, the library community has been amazing and very like open arms with programs and things like that and partnership opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: But really, yeah, Valone, the VFW, the American Legion, they've been amazing for us as far as the claims side goes.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about trauma treatment needs unique to LGBTQ+ veterans. Nicole identifies gaps in VA-provided services and mentions conversations with the Staten Island Pride Center about non-traditional therapies available to veterans.
chapters.title: Morano asks about unique trauma treatment needs for LGBTQ veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions related to mental health and health care in general, including the bill that we're hearing today that I've introduced.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Commissioner, your testimony referenced discrimination, social isolation, military sexual trauma, and mental health challenges.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Are there trauma treatment needs that are unique to LGBTQ plus veterans that aren't being adequately addressed by existing programs?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we believe that there are different challenges that are unique to this community that are not being addressed by the services available by the VA, especially given some of the policies that have came out.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We were in close conversation specifically.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: with the Staten Island Pride Center about the different therapies that are available that are non-traditional in the sense that this may not be something that the VA is actively promoting, that they're able to leverage even, you know, even though it's not veteran specific, they're able to utilize it.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So those are conversations that we have started in regards to different treatments that may be available that maybe the VA isn't providing.

chapters.summary: Morano asks if DVS has encountered veterans who might benefit from RTM therapy. Nicole says DVS believes veterans could benefit but, as a non-healthcare agency, believes the pilot study would be non-feasible for DVS and suggests Health and Hospitals as a better partner.
chapters.title: Morano asks about RTM therapy and better-suited agency for pilot

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Have you encountered veterans who might benefit from additional evidence-based therapies such as reconsolidation of traumatic memories?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We believe that there are veterans that can benefit from this.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: However, in regards to this bill, since we are not a health care provider, we believe that the pilot study would be non-feasible for us as a city agency, but we are supportive of any benefits that can help our veteran community.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Is there an agency that might better be suited?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We believe that collaboration with health and hospitals could be key for this.

chapters.summary: Morano asks if DVS differentiates PTSD treatment by trauma type. Nicole explains it depends on severity and crisis level, with sustained support evaluated based on eligibility criteria. Noah describes how claims agents connect veterans to specialized mental health partners.
chapters.title: Morano asks about PTSD treatment differentiation by trauma type

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: When DVS makes referrals for veterans with PTSD, does the agency differentiate between PTSD treatment focused on combat-related trauma

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: and trauma related to military training or anything else?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: It really depends on the severity of the case because it depends on where someone is in that crisis because at the end of the day, we want to just get them that support.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: But then when we're thinking about if someone needs that sustained support or is looking for more long-term solutions, that is something that programs are evaluated based on what the eligibility criteria would be to make the best determination of what would be able to help the veteran.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I'm going to pass over to Noah.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So as it relates to claims, that would come out of the conversation depending on if they disclose like a PTSD situation.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Claims goes a certain way, but then it also opens up the conversation about which partner we would connect them to that maybe specializes in whatever that they went through, whether that's a VA or a vet center.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Or just one of our mental health partners.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So again, it's very individualistic, but the conversation from the claims agent helps connect them to the specific partner.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about coordination with DOHMH and Health + Hospitals. Nicole describes connections to Pride Health Centers in each borough except Staten Island, providing local LGBTQ+ affirming healthcare access for veterans.
chapters.title: Morano asks about coordination with DOHMH and Health + Hospitals

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Since you guys alluded to health and hospitals a minute ago, does DVS coordinate with the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene or health and hospitals to connect LGBTQ plus veterans with health care services?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Assuming the answer to that is yes.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What does that collaboration actually look like?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so we are working very closely with both health and hospitals and Department of Health on this issue, as there are pride health centers in each of the boroughs except Staten Island, where we are connecting our veteran community over to in a more local, in a local way.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about health disparities requiring additional attention. Nicole identifies increased chronic illness among LGBTQ+ veterans and the need for more accessible HIV and STI testing through expanded partnerships.
chapters.title: Morano asks about health disparities among LGBTQ veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Are there particular health disparities?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Among LGBTQ plus veterans that DVS believes require additional attention?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: In addition to what the data shows us about there being an increase in chronic illness that is faced by LGBTQ plus veterans, and in our conversations with the Pride Center as well,

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: we see that there's definitely that need for HIV and STV testing that may not be as readily accessible by some of the larger organizations.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So we believe that the programs that focus on this is something that we are working to build out more of those partnerships for, so there's direct connection.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about efforts to connect military sexual trauma survivors to trauma-informed services. Noah describes claims agent training to identify triggers and connect veterans to specialized mental health partners. Nicole highlights coordination with VA MST coordinators and the mayor's office to end gender-based violence.
chapters.title: Morano asks about military sexual trauma services

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Given the high rates of military sexual trauma that I alluded to earlier that's been reported among LGBTQ plus veterans, what efforts has DVS undertaken to ensure survivors are connected to trauma-informed services?

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Oh, sure.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Just that goes back again to just having those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So especially with our claims agents, making sure they get the training that they need to kind of identify certain, I would say like a trigger word, which may lead them down a different conversation.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So depending on what they're speaking about and that rapport that they build,

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: We've built a bench as an agency with a lot of different mental health partners.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: And with each, I don't want to say flavor, but each one has a specialization that can deal with a certain, I would say like an incident in a different way.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So through those conversations, it really allows us to kind of put that veteran, connect that veteran to that very specific mental health partner.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I also do want to highlight that we work very closely with the VA MST coordinators.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So there's a coordinator in each VA facility, as well as we work very closely with the mayor's office to end gender-based violence.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And we just want to highlight that that is not a service that is just available to women veterans, but to all veterans.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about reducing stigma for MST survivors including LGBTQ+ veterans and male victims. Mata discusses humanizing veterans, creating awareness through social media, and letting veterans know it's okay to seek help. Nicole emphasizes nontraditional engagement like service animal events.
chapters.title: Morano asks about reducing stigma for MST survivors

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I was a bit surprised in my preparation for this hearing when the committee pointed out to me that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Post-Don't-Ask-Don't-Tell LGBTQ plus veterans are dealing with similarly high rates of

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: PTSD, social isolation, and that whole basket of maladies as their pre-Don't-Ask-Don't-Tell predecessors.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And as we dug deeper into this,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Apparently, part of the reason was there are some cultural issues that linger in the military and maybe even in the veteran community.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: What is DVS doing to reduce stigma about seeking care for survivors of military sexual trauma, including victims of military sexual trauma who are LGBTQ plus and victims who are men?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: At DBS, what we are doing differently is creating awareness of what veterans go through.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Veterans go through many, have gone through many hardships, very different.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And at DBS, if you look at our social medias, you are seeing us opening up conversations that normally would not be happening.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And when it comes to the LGBTQ+, what we want to do is

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: create that awareness of the services, one, that we provide, but also to let the LGBTQ plus veterans know that DVS

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: is a welcoming agency for them to be able to reach out to, but also that they know that they're going to be getting the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But most importantly is just humanizing the veteran.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Oftentimes, like, people feel that veterans don't need help because, you know, in their mindset, they think that they're strong.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And veterans are strong.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We're strong.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: But we're also...

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: human, right?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So our goal is to really humanize veterans themselves and also to, again, let them know that it's okay to seek help.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It's okay to say that you are not okay.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: That is what we are doing at DVS, highlight the

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I mean, to create that awareness, but also do it throughout various other agencies as well to create that partnership.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Because I think oftentimes other agencies may feel that because they're not veteran designated per se, that they're not supporting veterans, when in reality,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Again, that's why we asked for that question to be added because they are also supporting in their own way veterans in various other services.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And I think also in addition to that, and Commissioner alluded to it as well, but working with the nontraditional partners in a way that, you know, it's putting the services out there.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: It's letting them know that there's a space, but also not being overly like this is something that you have to sign up for.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: This is something that you should be doing.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I know my favorite groups at events are the vet dogs and the service animals because I think that that's a way where, you know, it brings everyone together.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And I think if we can just make sure that they know that we're here and it may not be now that they're reaching out for services, but the fact that they can down the line, that's what's important.

chapters.summary: Morano asks which city agencies DVS most frequently coordinates with for LGBTQ+ veterans. Nicole identifies the mayor's office to end gender-based violence for MST cases, plus DHS and DSS for housing. Donnie Smalls describes his direct line to the gender-based violence office and creating safe spaces for veterans.
chapters.title: Morano asks about inter-agency coordination for LGBTQ veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: A couple of questions before we wrap up related to your coordination with other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Which city agencies does DVS most frequently coordinate with regarding services for LGBT?

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: TQ plus veterans and what form does that coordination generally take?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that is specifically for LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We definitely see more connection with the mayor's office to end gender based violence when we're thinking about those cases for military sexual trauma.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And that usually comes from a warm handoff from our staff members right over to the office.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think more in general, what we're seeing is that LGBTQ plus veterans, in addition to just discharge upgrades and some benefits there, they're still reaching out to access housing services or claim support or just other services that veterans are reaching out for.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that's increased coordination with DHS, with DSS.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And I know that Donna could probably speak a little bit more to those referrals.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Yes, I work closely with the mayor's office to end gender-based violence.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I have a direct line of communication.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: So if any veteran is coming in and they express any type of assault or once they feel, I always tell my team to

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: create a safe space, hear what the veteran is not saying so that they can understand what they're really going through.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: And once they hear like certain keywords and they express, you know, privately things that have happened, you know, we kind of elaborate and let them know like we're also veterans.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I'm a veteran.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: A lot of times my staff come and get me.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: They have certain situations they need, you know, for the help with.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: And I just let them know, like, hey, I'm a veteran as well.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I've experienced different things in the military, just like you.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I'm no different.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Just because I'm on this side of the table doesn't mean that I can't, I can't, I don't understand what you're going through.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: So it's just about giving a person a safe space.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: Once they get that safe space, I let them know that.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I want to help as much as I can.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: I want to be able to get you the help that you need.

utterances.speaker: Donnie Smalls
utterances.text: So I'm able to call, you know, the wonderful staff over at the mayor's office and the base violence to get them that help and security that they need.

chapters.summary: Mata discusses partnerships with the Department of Aging and Disability to support the increasing aging veteran population and veterans with disabilities, noting both commissioners are from Staten Island.
chapters.title: Mata on aging and disability partnerships for veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And to further elaborate on the partnerships or relationships that we have with other agencies, at DBS we understand that there's an increasing aging population of veterans,

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: and also when it comes to disabilities as well.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So what we are doing is creating those strong partnerships with the Department of Aging and Disability.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: It just so happens the Commissioner for Disability as well as from Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So we are working very closely with both commissioners to ensure that we can support veterans holistically.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Taking over the city.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: That's it.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And we may soon have a flag if the hearing in the next room goes well.

chapters.summary: Morano asks about federal and state policy changes DVS is monitoring. Nicole identifies the trans ban with evolving discharge guidelines, the abortion ban in VA hospitals, and proposed cuts to sleep apnea and tinnitus benefits in the Take Care of America's Veterans Act.
chapters.title: Morano asks about federal and state policy changes affecting LGBTQ veterans

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Are there any federal or state policy changes that DVS is monitoring that could affect LGBTQ plus veterans, I guess, with the exception of the certificate of eligibility, which we've already covered?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: Yeah, so the trans ban remains something that we're continuing to monitor as different guidelines coming out for different times of discharge and what qualifications may allow someone to stay in temporarily.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that is something that we're actively monitoring.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: We're also actively monitoring the abortion ban in VA hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that's something else that we're tracking.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And then more in general, less specific on LGBTQ plus issues is in the Take Care of America's Veterans Act.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: There's some proposed legislation that would cut sleep apnea and tinnitus benefits over a period of 10 years.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: That is something that we are actively tracking as well, which there's many veterans in the community who would be affected by that.

chapters.summary: Morano asks what the most urgent challenge is and what action the council should take. Nicole identifies discharge upgrades and sustained funding for legal service providers as most urgent. Mata emphasizes the need for better data systems to identify and serve LGBTQ+ veterans.
chapters.title: Morano asks about most urgent challenge and recommended council action

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Let me, I'll end with this.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I mean, part of the reason that we chose this specific topic is because it's Pride Month.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I'd love to come back a year from now and see how we've done.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: The committee, me as a council member, the council as a body, you guys as an agency.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Of all the challenges that are facing LGBTQ plus veterans in New York City today, which one, in your opinion, is the most urgent and what specific action should the city council take to address it?

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think for us, discharge upgrades and access to those services are the most primary focus.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: I think also the providers that provide these services are definitely, there's the question about funding and sustained funding.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: So that is something that we view as more urgent as we understand that this issue can only primarily be dealt with by lawyers.

utterances.speaker: Nicole
utterances.text: And that is something that we are really tracking to ensure that if we do see that influx, which we hope of more people reaching out for their benefits, there's that support that will meet them.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And what we hope to do is to have, again, better systems in place to ensure that veterans, one, not only self-identify, but also LGBTQ plus veterans can self-identify.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: I really look forward to having further conversations with Councilmember Carr.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We came up with various ideas.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We would like also want part of the ideas to hold meetings with LGBTQ plus veterans so we can work together.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And if the census itself doesn't ask the LGBTQ.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Plus questionnaire, then how can we do it collectively for LGBTQ plus veterans, but so they can get the support that they need.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So we are, again, we are exploring what data collection might may look like for LGBTQ plus veterans, but in general, general for all veterans to ensure that holistically they're fully supported.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So I do hope that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: as well one year from now, DVS does have better data systems in place.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So we can, again, I could just come in here and say this is how many LGBTQ plus veterans we served and this is how many veterans, LGBTQ plus veterans are in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: So I do hope a year from now we are in a better place.

chapters.summary: Wong asks how often DVS meets with federal veteran services officers on Houston Street. Noah Bray reports liaising with them almost daily and visiting the Houston Street office at least every two weeks.
chapters.title: Wong asks about meeting with federal veteran services officers

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Just a final question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How often do you meet with the federal veteran services officers in, say, in Houston Street?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And what?

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: On the federal level, I see them at almost every outreach event that we go to.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: They're very good.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Their outreach component is great.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: So I liaise with them on an almost daily basis.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: House and street, I visit at least every two weeks to talk to my partner.

utterances.speaker: Noah Bray
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Morano thanks all four DVS representatives for their testimony, energy, and nonpartisan advocacy on behalf of New York City's veteran community.
chapters.title: Morano wraps up administration testimony, thanks DVS team

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Well, thank you, all four of you, not only for your testimony today, but for your

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: energy and advocacy on behalf of New York veterans and someone that gets to work pretty closely with all of you and your agency.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Thank you for your partnership and for your nonpartisan approach to helping New York City's veteran community.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Appreciate it very much.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Thank you, Council Member.

chapters.summary: Morano opens the floor for public testimony, reminding attendees of formal proceeding rules including silence, no video recording from the witness table, three-minute time limit, and written testimony submission procedures.
chapters.title: Morano opens public testimony with rules and procedures

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Now we are going to open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And I'll remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Quorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: And the witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings as testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the Sergeant-at-Arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing and you haven't already done so, please fill out an appearance card, look like this, with the sergeant at arms, and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: When recognized, you will have three minutes to speak on today's hearing topic, programs and services for LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: We'll hear all in-person testimony first and then turn to testimony on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Yesenia Mata
utterances.text: If you have a...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the Sergeant at Arms, and I can assure you

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: that not only does the committee staff read all that testimony and review it as we shape future hearing topics and future legislative topics, but I read it all, so feel free to submit it as you like.

chapters.summary: Morano calls the first public panel: Brian Ellicott-Cook from SAGE, Amelia Cobbs from the New York Health Foundation, and Coco Culhane from the Veteran Advocacy Project.
chapters.title: First public panel called: SAGE, NY Health Foundation, VAP

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right, I will now call the first panel.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Very, very pleased to welcome yet another Staten Islanders.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We're taking over.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Brian Ellicott-Cook.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: He's with Sage.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I want to welcome Amelia Cobbs with the New York Health Foundation and a fan favorite, Coco Culhane of the Veteran Advocacy Project.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Please come on down.

chapters.summary: Brian Ellicott-Cook of SAGE testifies about the SAGE Vets program serving LGBTQ+ veterans age 50+, sharing stories of veterans who found community, housing support, and grief processing. He reports 118 programs with 4,000+ attendees in the past year and supports both Intro 914 (with amendments) and Intro 928.
chapters.title: Brian Ellicott-Cook (SAGE) testifies on LGBTQ veteran programs and impact

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right, Brian, please begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Probably the only council member who's ever said my last name correctly.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Moreno, members of the Committee on Veterans.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: My name is Brian Ellicott-Cook, and I serve as the Director of Government Relations at SAGE, the nation's largest and oldest organization dedicated to improving the lives of LGBTQ plus older adults.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: I am also a proud member of a Marine and FDNY family, and my connection to this work is deeply personal.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: I previously led SAGE Vets, our program serving LGBTQ plus veterans, and it remains one of the highlights and most meaningful parts of my career.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Sage strongly supports Intro 914, previously by Chair Holden.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: It needs some work and amendments, and I'm happy to talk to you about that more, but I want to save my testimony for the subject at hand.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: And we very much support Intro 928 from Councilmember Carr to establish an LGBT plus liaison in the Department of Veterans Services, both critical steps towards visibility and equitable support.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: At SAGE, we serve LGBTQ plus veterans age 50 and older across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Over the past year alone, we have hosted 118 programs with more than 4,000 attendees, demonstrating not just participation, but consistent engagement for veterans seeking connection and support.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: But the real impact in the...

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Is in the lives behind these numbers.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: And I'm going to read a couple of testimonies of those veterans.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: One veteran, JL, a 57-year-old Navy veteran, came to us experiencing trauma and discrimination during his service.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Through our program, he found both practical support, like helping navigating discharge upgrades and creative writing group, where he was safely able to process his experiences.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: He shared that for the first time, he has had a space where he could fully express himself, both as a veteran and an LGBTQ plus person.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Another person known as Mr.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: I is a 78-year-old Army veteran living with Parkinson's.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Came to us facing harassment in his housing.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: We supported him in securing safe housing, stabilizing his situation.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Through our nutrition and wellness program, he was able to better manage his health, and he is consistently telling us how important it is to be in a space where he feels respected and affirmed.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: And Mr.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: L., an 84-year-old veteran, came to us during a period of profound grief after losing his partner.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Through our weekly support groups and community outings, he has found connection again, describing the experience as grounding, restorative during one of the most difficult times of his life.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: These stories show what's possible when LGBTQ plus veterans are met with consistency, dignity, and culturally responsive care.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: We are proud of what Sage Vets has built, but more importantly, we are proud of the veterans we continue to show up for and support one another and rebuild the community.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: There's a lot more data in my longer testimony, but I appreciate your time.

utterances.speaker: Brian Ellicott-Cook
utterances.text: Thank you, Brian.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Well done.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Amelia, if I can call you Amelia, I hope you don't mind the first name.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: No problem.

chapters.summary: Amelia Cobbs of the New York Health Foundation testifies about incomplete data collection, worse mental health outcomes for queer veterans, and a suicide rate more than twice the overall veteran population. She notes recent VA policy changes ending gender identity initiatives and the closure of the NYU Military Family Clinic, urging the city to close data gaps and strengthen outreach.
chapters.title: Amelia Cobbs (NY Health Foundation) testifies on health disparities and data gaps

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: My name is Amelia Cobbs, Policy and Research Associate at the New York Health Foundation.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Committed to improving the health of all New Yorkers, including our city's roughly 120,000 veterans.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairperson Murano and members of the committee for the opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Over the years, we have had the opportunity to partner closely with city agencies and stakeholders to ensure New York's veterans receive high-quality, culturally competent care and support, including LGBTQ plus veterans.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Our research has found that data collection across veteran populations is often incomplete and inconsistent, including for queer veterans and veterans with multiple identities.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Veterans of this community experience worse mental health outcomes, partially due to chronic exposure to discrimination, social exclusion, and barriers to care.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: These barriers lead to increased suicide risk, and the national suicide rate among queer veterans is more than twice that of the overall veteran population.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: New York City has been a leader in care for the queer community.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: The city's DVS has developed multiple programs supporting queer veterans, including an LGBT veteran liaison and VetConnect NYC, which connects veterans to needed resources.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Nationally, the VA has become an

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: national leader in healthcare and cultural competency for queer veterans over the past decade.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: However, recent federal policy changes have created an unsafe environment for veterans in the queer community.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Reports indicate that supports and therapies for queer veterans within some VA facilities have been reduced.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: And just two weeks ago on June 12th,

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: The VA ordered all facilities to end gender identity-based initiatives and remove LGBTQ plus designations from medical coordinator networks.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: And as you know, the Military Family Clinic at NYU-Lingon will be closing this September.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: This program provides free mental health care, and its closure will impact queer veterans'access to care.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: We have specific recommendations listed in our testimony, but broadly, we urge the city to close critical data gaps, expand veteran identification and screening in non-VA settings,

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: strengthen outreach and engagement, and strengthen peer support programming.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: And we're happy to discuss more about how to support queer veterans in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: In addition, we have forthcoming reports on suicides and deaths of despair in New York City, which we are happy to share prior to publication.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Thank you for your time.

chapters.summary: Morano asks Cobbs if there are health care access issues the committee hasn't touched on. Cobbs emphasizes the importance of the city remaining a safe environment and keeping queer veterans a priority population, offering to follow up with colleagues.
chapters.title: Morano questions Cobbs on additional health care gaps

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'd love to take a look at those reports prior to publication.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Just a quick question.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I appreciate all the work that went into your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I'm certainly eager to use the New York Health Foundation as a partner and a resource going forward.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: You focused on a lot of the things that we spoke about today, the gap in data, other things of that nature.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Is there anything that you think that we haven't touched upon today that...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Would better help the LGBT community get access to health care and health care-related services that they're not currently getting.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Anything we're missing as a committee or based on the folks that have testified so far?

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: I would say that I think we've really touched on so many important topics for queer veterans.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: I think it's just the idea that the city continues to be a safe environment and that queer veterans remain a priority population.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: And if there are any more specific ideas, I would love to get back to you with conversations with my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Amelia Cobbs
utterances.text: Please.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Ms.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Colhane, hello.
