chapters.summary: Coco Culhane of the Veteran Advocacy Project testifies in support of DVS programs but strongly opposes Intro 914, citing poor research quality, bias in RTM studies, lack of peer-reviewed evidence, conflicts of interest involving Dr. Burke, and the closure of evidence-based programs while city funds go to unproven therapies.
chapters.title: Coco Culhane (VAP) testifies against Intro 914 and on discharge upgrade barriers

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: I'm Coco Colhane, Director of Veteran Advocacy Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: We provide free legal services to veterans with mental health conditions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Just to touch on the bill, we are in support and are very happy to hear that there are already so many programs for LGBTQIA plus veterans at DVS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: One thing I just wanted to...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: is that there's a State Restoration of Honor Act, and it excludes veterans who have punitive discharges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And that we see older vets who are charged under a sodomy statute or who were persecuted for their sexual orientation, but under the guise of misconduct.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And so some of them do have these bad conduct discharges.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: New York State says, no, that's proper.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And I think that's re-injuring.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: At the city level, we include those individuals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And I think that's terrific and encourage and support anything else that DVS is doing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Also just wanted to note that right now transgender individuals, there's a court injunction, so they're not being discharged.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: They're not allowed to enlist, but it's up in the air what will happen.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And then we just wanted to note the population that we primarily work with has a suicide rate that's three times that of other veterans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And from what I've been told, three of the largest programs, one of them being NYU, are closed or closing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And so the mental health care needs are just...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Astounding and alarm bells I think should be going off, but it's against that backdrop that VAP strongly opposes Intro 914 for any number of reasons, which I'll just go through a few.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Of the very small amount of research that's been done, almost all of it has been

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: conducted by its inventor, its creator, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Burke.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: There was an analysis done of the four studies, and it said that their documentation suggested bias.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: They didn't assess symptoms beyond two weeks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: There were insufficient data to draw any conclusions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: The one study that was not led by Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Burke compares veterans who received RTM to veterans on a wait list.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: With no treatment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: So not exactly a control group.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And DOD and VA have both cited to a lack of evidence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Last summer, there was a piece in the Atlantic Magazine about this.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: When the journalist asked Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Bork why so many academics say that point to his studies being of such poor quality, he didn't dispute it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: He said in order to attract the interest, support, and funding from prestigious universities and laboratories and researchers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: So I think instead they've attracted some people at city council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: From just going back over the years, my understanding is that this referrals were being made and training was facilitated pre-pandemic under the leadership of Brigadier General Sutton.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And, you know, in a 2023 hearing, she came with Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Bork, a client, and Ed Shulman, and they had, can I?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: They had over an hour to present, really sell RTM to the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And one of the things that was...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: said that there was a 90% success rate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: But if you look again at any of the peer-reviewed, any of the academic reviews and critiques of this, it's one, I'll just quote,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: RTM patients were only people who were compared to people receiving no treatment at all.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Any form of treatment would be better than nothing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: That's unsurprising in this context.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Even 90% improvement doesn't mean much.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Then at a 2024 hearing, former chair Holden characterized that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: training was being done and RTM was sort of sweeping the nation and that there were doctors in every community, that this was an accepted thing and it was great.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: If you go and look on their website, there are 297 providers in the United States and only seven of them are doctors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: That's just going on their own data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And, you know, I think that it's important for us to look at who is pushing these things, who, you know, in 2022, the American Legion of New York gave a $100,000 grant to study

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: RTM, and it was given to a nonprofit, which I don't have right in front of me, but the nonprofit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Turns out is run by Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Bork.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: So you're having someone whose trademark therapy is a for-profit entity within, I mean, it's a confusing structure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: There's a nonprofit and a for-profit.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And then last year,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: My understanding, if you look at Schedule C, $50,000 was given to Operation Warrior Shield to do a study, to provide treatment and do a study.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: So where are the results?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Why don't we look at that before moving forward with anything else?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And I'll leave it there.

chapters.summary: Morano questions Culhane on why a pilot study would be problematic given it would generate data. Culhane argues the city is already funding RTM, evidence-based programs are shutting doors, and no academic institution is willing to fund RTM research, questioning why the city should fund what experts won't.
chapters.title: Morano questions Culhane on RTM pilot study rationale

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I really appreciate your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I think you might have missed my remarks at the beginning of the hearing is even though I'm introducing this bill, I don't necessarily have a dog in the fight.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'm really just reintroducing a bill that the prior chair.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Put in and I have no interest in pushing forward with forcing a treatment to be better recognized in New York City if it's not effective.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: That being said, the bill itself

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It is a pilot program and a study that leads to a pilot program.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What's wrong with that, right?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Why wouldn't that just give us more data?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And if it turns out to be, and I'm not saying it is, but if it turns out to be something concocted by a charlatan looking to make money, then we know, okay,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: it stops there and potentially helps other cities or other state or federal agencies as they consider RTM in the future.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: What's wrong with a pilot?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: program and the study that leads to a pilot program?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Yeah, I would just say, one, we're already doing it, right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: I mean, there's something undergoing right now with city council money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: But number two, when we have programs that have evidence-backed scientific data of their efficacy that are shutting their doors, and there's such huge need,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: why would we then go fund something that no academic institution seems willing to fund?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And I am not, you know, I've talked about RTM a few times in testimony, and I'm not a doctor, I'm not a mental health care provider, so this is just based on the logic of research we've looked at and just being concerned about our clients'needs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And not an attack, just a deep concern given the various ties and the lack of data.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Are there other treatments that aren't currently being prioritized in New York City that would be more effective than RTM?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And what do you think those might be?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: So, again, I don't want to, I'm not qualified.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Right, I understand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: Right?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: But, I mean, usual CBT, I don't prolong exposure, is controversial.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: But there are various methods and different programs prioritize one over the other.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And so we just refer out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Coco Culhane
utterances.text: And let veterans choose what they, you know, and speak with their providers about what's going to work best for them.

chapters.summary: Morano asks Culhane about alternative treatments that should be prioritized. Culhane defers on medical expertise but mentions CBT and prolonged exposure as established options, emphasizing the importance of letting veterans choose with their providers.
chapters.title: Morano asks Culhane about alternative PTSD treatments

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Colhane, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Joking, for the record.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you very, very much.

chapters.summary: Joe Bellow of NY Metro Vets testifies with concerns about both bills. On Intro 914, he argues DVS is not a healthcare agency and evaluating medical modalities opens a Pandora's box. On Intro 928, he questions why one community gets a codified liaison while others don't, and asks what the liaison would do that DVS doesn't already do.
chapters.title: Joe Bellow (NY Metro Vets) testifies on concerns with Intro 914 and 928

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Let me welcome up to the witness table both Joe Bellow of New York Metro Vets and Timothy Pena of the Veterans Justice Project.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And as they make their way up here, thank you, all three of you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: As they make their way up here, I'll just note, and I'm sure this will upset a lot of people, that I am really appreciative of not only Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Bellow and Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Pena coming to just about every Veterans Committee hearing and preparing to offer testimony, but being so vocal in advocating for the veterans community, including when that

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: vocalism might be critical of things that the committee is doing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I would much rather be criticized by folks that have the best interests of veterans at heart and are putting in so much time, effort, and work in trying to improve the plight of veterans than get praise from people that don't do anything.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: With that, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Bellow, take it away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Chair Marano, members of the Veterans Community, thank you for the opportunity to testify at today's Oversight Hearing on Support and Programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Let me begin by saying I do not intend to offer testimony regarding the specific needs, experiences, or challenges facing LGBTQ veterans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: I am not the most qualified person to speak on those issues, and I would actually defer to members of the community and organizations with expertise in the area, such as Brian Elikok.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: I will focus my testimony on intros 914 and 928 and explain my concern with both bills.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Starting with intro 914, my concern is not whether RTM is a good treatment or a bad treatment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: I think Coco kind of expressed that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: My concern is why the city, and specifically the Department of Veteran Services, is being asked to evaluate a specific medical modality.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: DVS is not a health care agency, a research institution, or a medical regulator.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Its mission is to connect veterans with benefits, services, and support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Evaluating medical treatments falls outside of that mission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: RTM has its issues, but there are also many other emerging therapies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: The Department of Veterans Affairs is currently conducting and supporting research into a variety of treatments for PTSD and other serious mental health conditions, including MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD and alcohol use disorder.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: If the city conducts a feasibility,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: study on RTM today, what is the rationale for not studying other emergent therapies tomorrow?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Where do we draw the line?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: That is why I believe this bill opens a Pandora's box.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Once the city begins evaluating individual treatment modalities, it will inevitably face requests to evaluate others as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Former Commissioner Hendon raised similar concerns during the hearings that Coco and I were at last year regarding the prior version of this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: He noted both the financial and operational challenges involved and stated that the Department of Health's longstanding position is that the city does not validate specific health care modalities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: I believe that remains the correct approach.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: The city should focus on connecting veterans to available care, not determining which therapies deserve special recognition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: With regards to Intro 928, as former Councilmember Paul Vallone often said, we, the city, cannot legislate everything.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: DVS has a responsibility to serve all veterans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: If there are gaps in outreach and access to services for LBGTQ veterans at DVS, as was said today, those gaps should be identified and addressed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: However, I'm not convinced that creating a statutory liaison position is the answer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: For years, advocates, including myself, have requested a dedicated Veterans Advisory Board liaison within DVS, yet that legislation and proposal never moved forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Why is the liaison for one specific segment of the veterans community now being placed in the administrative code?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: This proposal also raises broader questions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: If we create legislatively mandated liaisons for LBGTQ veterans,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: will future councils be asked to create liaisons for women veterans, African American veterans, Hispanic veterans, Asian American veterans, disabled veterans, or other groups within the veteran community?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: So my question listening to that testimony was, what else does Donna do besides being the liaison at DVS?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: What's her primary job?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: The bill also leaves important practical questions unanswered.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Would this require a new hire?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Would these responsibilities be assigned to an existing employee?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: As I was saying, what exactly would this liaison do that DVS is not already expected to do?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: How would success be measured?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Veterans come to DVS first and foremost as veterans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: The department should focus on increasing the intake, better intake, and remain on ensuring that all veterans have equal access to benefits, services, and support without creating additional bureaucratic structures or precedents that may

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: prove difficult to sustain in the future.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Finally, I would encourage this committee to look beyond proposals carried over from the prior council and focus on broader policy initiatives that would improve how DVS serves veterans and their families throughout New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Morano questions Bellow on Intro 928, asking what mechanism would protect LGBTQ+ liaisons if a future administration eliminated them. Bellow questions whether codifying one liaison opens the door for every subgroup to demand their own, and emphasizes veterans should be served as veterans first.
chapters.title: Morano questions Bellow on Intro 928 codification concerns

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: First, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Bellow, thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'll note that we are looking at other things, too, including inspired by a lot of the work that you do and have done in your newsletter and a lot of your work.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Your observations.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I totally understand where you're coming from on Intro 914.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: A quick question on Intro 928.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So you noted that DVS, and this was covered when I spoke with the commissioner, already has two LGBTQ plus liaisons and questioned whether that function should be codified.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If a future administration eliminated those positions tomorrow, what mechanism would exist

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: to ensure that LGBTQ plus veterans continue to have a designated point of contact and advocate within the agency?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Well, I think this goes back to the first question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: So they have two LBGTQ liaisons.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: My understanding from what I heard, one is outreach, one is claims.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: So my question would be, where is that on DVS's website?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: First and foremost, I think, I mean, maybe it's there, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: But I think that this goes back to what I said in my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: If we're doing, if a veteran comes to DVS as a veteran and it's within the intake and within the conversation that is identified as LBGTQ, do they not have somebody that's...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: that's they have there that's an additional that made that would first I mean if that's an additional duty that's fine but I just think that if we're gonna codify something into a another position then that opens it up to like you know

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: I'm a disabled veteran.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Where's my liaison?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: You know, I just think that they, again, the way I see it and the way it's said to me through my conversations with veterans, anybody who goes to DVS comes in as a veteran or a family member.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Mr. Bellow
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Noted.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: It is on the website, by the way, from what I could tell.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Pena, thank you.

chapters.summary: Timothy Pena of the Veterans Justice Project testifies as a veteran living with HIV who experienced homelessness. He describes fear of disclosure at Borden Avenue Veterans Residence, stigma, 1,365 emergency calls in 16 months, and argues that privacy, dignity, trust, and accountability are as important as housing for vulnerable LGBTQ+ veterans.
chapters.title: Timothy Pena (Veterans Justice Project) testifies on stigma, housing, and fear of disclosure

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Take it away.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Good morning, Chairman Moreno, members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: My name is Timothy Pena.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I'm a disabled United States veteran, founder of Veterans Justice Project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I speak today as a veteran who experienced homelessness,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: as a person living with HIV,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: and as someone concerned about the growing barriers preventing vulnerable LGBTQ plus veterans from seeking help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I in part left the military as a 20-year-old because I was being sexually molested.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I often hear questions about why so many veterans fail to self-identify or refuse services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: My experience tells me the answer is simple.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: We're afraid.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: When I arrived in New York, I spent five months at a Borden Avenue Veterans residence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: During that time, I interacted with more than a dozen caseworkers across the VA, HUDVASH, HASA, DHS, and the Department of Veterans Services and other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Yet despite all those resources, I frequently felt alone, confused, and fearful.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: As a veteran living with HIV, Mo, every conversation involving housing meant disclosing sensitive medical information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Veterans seeking HASA,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Support housing vouchers are afforded very little privacy in a congregate setting such as Borden Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Everyone knows everyone else's business.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Rumors spread quickly, and veterans are left wondering whether the next person who learns something about them will use it against them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I consider myself heterosexual, yet merely being HIV positive creates opportunities for people to make assumptions and then act upon those prejudices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Stigma still exists.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Those fears are not their own.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Erotical.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Theoretical.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Just two months ago, another veteran told me that the only reason I have an apartment is because I have AIDS.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It's an ignorant statement, dehumanizing, and absolutely not true.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: But that's the world that I'm stuck in.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It has erased years of struggle and suggested that housing was somehow handed to me because of an illness.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Fear of disclosure also affects health care.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Veterans may avoid seeking treatment through the VA or asking questions about available programs because they fear someone in the shelter will find out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Many veterans are either not properly informed about services or are simply too afraid to ask questions because they perceive staff as apathetic or doubt whether they will be protected.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Continue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: During my five months at Borden Avenue, I lost 24 pounds.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I witnessed assaults, overdoses, veterans sleeping outside because they feared for their safety.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I saw visible injuries on residents in an environment that I believed was harmful to recovery and mental health.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Subsequent records have shown approximately 1,365 emergency calls to Borden Avenue between August 2024 and December 2025.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: In that 16 months, it's involving assaults, emotionally disturbed persons,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: injuries, unconscious individuals, and suspected overdoses.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: These are not the conditions that vulnerable veterans should be forced to endure while they are also trying to deal with things like I have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: At the same time, reductions in LGBTQ plus

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Related programs and protections within the Department of Veterans Affairs send a troubling message to veterans already reluctant to come forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Symbolism matters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: If veterans do not believe they will be treated with dignity, they simply stop asking for help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I have felt attacked by this committee when a council member once told me to go back to Phoenix.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I have watched the veterans of

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: advisory board repeatedly minimize the concerns of homeless veterans.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I have experienced what I believe to be open disdain from Department of Veterans Services after raising concerns about the safety and welfare of the veterans back in 2022 when I first arrived.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: This still continues today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Yet I remain optimistic because I know New York City itself is better than that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I feel safe in my neighborhood and throughout this city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: What concerns me are not New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: It is whether the institutions charged with protecting LGBTQ veterans are truly listening to those most at risk.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: I don't believe they are.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: If we are serious about reducing veteran homelessness, preventing suicide, then we must recognize that privacy, dignity, trust, and accountability are every bit as important as housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: No veteran should have to choose between seeking treatment and protecting themselves from these stigmas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: No veteran should have to fear violence because of a medical condition or because of their orientation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: And no veteran should have to suffer in silence because they no longer trust the institutions created to serve them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Timothy Pena
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you, Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Pena.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I'll note that I've never told you to go back to Phoenix, and I'm glad you're a New Yorker, and I value your perspective in these hearings very much.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

chapters.summary: Morano delivers closing remarks, thanking all testifiers and noting the low turnout of five public witnesses. He calls for broader veteran participation in future hearings, notes no current council member is a military veteran, and urges advocates to help spread the word about upcoming hearings on mental health and small business issues.
chapters.title: Morano closing remarks calling for broader veteran participation

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Thank you to everybody who came here to share their thoughts and experiences today.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If there's anyone in the room who wishes to speak but hasn't yet had the opportunity to do so, just raise your hand and fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms at the back of the room.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And we actually have nobody on Zoom at the moment, right?

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So if there's anyone else present in the room who has not had the opportunity to do so, but wishes to, just raise your hand and let us know.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Hands, I'd like to note again that members of the public can submit written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I'll just note to all the veterans advocates watching, either in person or online or on television, that,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: you know, we had five members of the public come testify today, including three people that are at every hearing that we do, and I'm grateful for their participation.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: This really is such an important issue, and I'd love to hear from either folks in the LGBT community or

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: in the veteran community about what I, as chair of this committee, can do to have a broader outreach effort and get more people participating.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Because I guarantee you there are more than five New Yorkers who could have offered substantive

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: feedback based on the administration's testimony today and substantive legislative suggestions, not only for the legislation that we're hearing today, but for future legislation concerning LGBTQ veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So to everybody watching, and I know a lot of people do watch these hearings, email me, morano at council.nyc.gov, and tell me what you think I could be doing to,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: I would love to have this hearing go on for five hours and fight with the chair of the New York City Council.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: next committee because we're not done yet hearing everybody's solutions.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: If we're hearing again and again from the same three or four veteran advocates, it's really difficult to move the ball forward for all of New York's veterans community.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I've talked about before that, unfortunately, while we have a couple of people that are military spouses serving in the city council and a couple of people that are children of veterans serving in the city council,

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: there is not a single member of the current New York City Council that is a military veteran themselves.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And I only mention that because...

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: there's a huge perspective that is not being heard.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And the only way we can fill that gap and fill that void in lacking a veteran perspective is if this witness table is filled with people like Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Pena and Mr.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Bellow and Ms.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Colhane and make sure that we're hearing what the needs of the veterans community are.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So I would ask.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Everybody in the veterans community, please help us spread the word about these hearings.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: We have a lot of very important hearings coming up that I hope is going to do very substantive work on mental health-related issues, on small business-related issues, specifically affecting veterans.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And we want to hear from you.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Sincerely, it's not me.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Reading a pro forma.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: speech.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: They've stopped scripting stuff for me because I go off script so much.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So let us know what we can do to reach out to your communities better.

chapters.summary: Morano thanks attendees, notes Councilmember Wong stayed for the entire hearing, and adjourns the hearing.
chapters.title: Hearing adjourned

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: So to conclude, I'm grateful to everyone that attended today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: Very, very grateful as well for the work of the committee staff and grateful, but not at all surprised that Councilmember Wong has stayed for every minute of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Frank Morano
utterances.text: And with that, this hearing is now adjourned.

summary: Oversight hearing on support and programs for LGBTQ+ veterans, covering data gaps, discharge upgrade challenges, mental health needs, and the impact of DADT and the 2025 trans ban.

Two bills heard: Intro 914 (study on reconsolidation of traumatic memories therapy for veterans) and Intro 928 (establishing an LGBTQ liaison within DVS), with DVS expressing support for 928 goals and noting feasibility concerns about 914.

Public testimony from SAGE, NY Health Foundation, Veteran Advocacy Project, NY Metro Vets, and Veterans Justice Project highlighted health disparities, research concerns about RTM therapy, and personal experiences of stigma and housing insecurity.
title: Committee on Veterans
topic: Oversight - Support and Programs for LGBTQ+ Veterans
